title: List All Airports in a Country
description: As an airline operations manager, you need to quickly identify all available airports within a specific region to plan new routes. Your task is to find all airports located in 'USA'. Retrieve the airport ID, name, city, and country for these airports.
solution: SELECT airport_id, name, city, country FROM airports WHERE country = 'USA';

title: Find Aircraft by Model
description: A maintenance team needs to schedule routine checks for all 'Boeing 737' aircraft in the fleet. To facilitate this, they require a list of all aircraft matching this model. Retrieve the aircraft ID, model, and capacity for all 'Boeing 737' aircraft.
solution: SELECT aircraft_id, model, capacity FROM aircraft WHERE model = 'Boeing 737';

title: Flights Departing from a Specific Airport
description: A ground control team at 'JFK' airport needs to see all upcoming flights scheduled to depart from their location. This helps them manage gate assignments and ground services. Retrieve all details for flights departing from 'JFK'.
solution: SELECT * FROM flights WHERE departure_airport_id = 'JFK';

title: Retrieve Flight Status History
description: An incident response team is investigating a particular flight, 'FL001', and needs to review its entire status history. This includes all reported statuses and their timestamps. Retrieve all status history entries for flight 'FL001'.
solution: SELECT * FROM flight_status_history WHERE flight_id = 'FL001';

title: List Crew Assignments for a Flight
description: A flight supervisor needs to confirm the crew manifest for flight 'FL002' before departure. This ensures all assigned personnel are accounted for. Retrieve the employee ID and role for all crew members assigned to flight 'FL002'.
solution: SELECT employee_id, role FROM crew_assignments WHERE flight_id = 'FL002';

title: Aircraft with High Capacity
description: For long-haul routes, the planning department needs to identify aircraft capable of carrying a large number of passengers. They are specifically looking for aircraft with a capacity greater than 200. Retrieve the aircraft ID, model, and capacity for these aircraft.
solution: SELECT aircraft_id, model, capacity FROM aircraft WHERE capacity > 200;

title: Unique Airport Cities
description: The marketing department is planning a campaign and wants to know all the unique cities where the airline operates. This helps them target their advertising efforts. Retrieve a distinct list of all cities where airports are located.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT city FROM airports;

title: Flights Arriving After a Specific Time
description: The night shift ground crew needs a list of all flights scheduled to arrive at any airport after 22:00:00 on '2024-06-01'. This helps them prepare for late-night operations. Retrieve the flight ID, departure and arrival airports, and scheduled arrival time for these flights.
solution: SELECT flight_id, departure_airport_id, arrival_airport_id, scheduled_arrival FROM flights WHERE scheduled_arrival > '2024-06-01 22:00:00';

title: Count Flights per Aircraft Model
description: The fleet management team wants to understand the utilization of different aircraft models. They need a count of how many flights each model has been assigned to. Retrieve the aircraft model and the total number of flights for each model. (Only include aircraft models that have been assigned to at least one flight). Name the result column 'total_flights'.
solution: SELECT a.model, COUNT(f.flight_id) AS total_flights FROM aircraft a JOIN flights f ON a.aircraft_id = f.aircraft_id GROUP BY a.model;

title: Total Capacity by Aircraft Model
description: For strategic planning, the airline needs to know the total passenger capacity available for each aircraft model in its fleet. This helps in route allocation and demand forecasting. Retrieve the aircraft model and the sum of capacity for all aircraft of that model. Name the sum column 'total_capacity'.
solution: SELECT model, SUM(capacity) AS total_capacity FROM aircraft GROUP BY model;

title: Airports with Departure Counts
description: Airport authorities want to analyze traffic patterns by identifying which airports handle the most departures. This data is crucial for resource allocation and expansion plans. Retrieve the airport name and the count of flights departing from each airport. (Only include airports that have at least one departing flight). Name the result column 'departure_count'.
solution: SELECT ap.name, COUNT(f.flight_id) AS departure_count FROM airports ap JOIN flights f ON ap.airport_id = f.departure_airport_id GROUP BY ap.name ORDER BY departure_count DESC;

title: Average Aircraft Capacity
description: The procurement department is evaluating new aircraft purchases and needs to know the average passenger capacity across the entire fleet. This helps in setting benchmarks for new acquisitions. Retrieve the average capacity of all aircraft. Name the result column 'average_capacity'.
solution: SELECT AVG(capacity) AS average_capacity FROM aircraft;

title: Flights with Airport Names
description: For a user-friendly flight schedule display, the system needs to show the full names of the departure and arrival airports instead of just their IDs. This improves readability for passengers. Retrieve the flight ID, the name of the departure airport, and the name of the arrival airport. Name the result column 'departure_airport_name'.
solution: SELECT f.flight_id, da.name AS departure_airport_name, aa.name AS arrival_airport_name FROM flights f JOIN airports da ON f.departure_airport_id = da.airport_id JOIN airports aa ON f.arrival_airport_id = aa.airport_id;

title: Count Crew Members per Flight
description: Flight operations need to ensure that each flight has the correct number of crew members assigned. They require a summary of how many crew members are assigned to each flight. Retrieve the flight ID and the count of crew members for each flight. Name the result column 'crew_count'.
solution: SELECT flight_id, COUNT(employee_id) AS crew_count FROM crew_assignments GROUP BY flight_id;

title: Latest Status for Each Flight
description: Air traffic controllers and airline dispatchers need to quickly see the most current status for every active flight. This helps in real-time decision-making. Retrieve the flight ID, the latest status, and the timestamp of that status for each flight. Name the result column 'max_timestamp'.
solution: SELECT fsh.flight_id, fsh.status, fsh.timestamp FROM flight_status_history fsh JOIN (SELECT flight_id, MAX(timestamp) AS max_timestamp FROM flight_status_history GROUP BY flight_id) AS latest_status ON fsh.flight_id = latest_status.flight_id AND fsh.timestamp = latest_status.max_timestamp;

title: Aircraft Models and Assigned Flight Count
description: The fleet management team wants to see which aircraft models are most frequently used. They need a list of all aircraft models and the total number of flights each model has been assigned to. Retrieve the aircraft model and the count of assigned flights, including models with no assigned flights (showing 0). Name the result column 'assigned_flights_count'.
solution: SELECT a.model, COUNT(f.flight_id) AS assigned_flights_count FROM aircraft a LEFT JOIN flights f ON a.aircraft_id = f.aircraft_id GROUP BY a.model ORDER BY assigned_flights_count DESC;

title: Date and Time Functions
description: SQLite stores dates as TEXT ISO8601 strings. You can use the `strftime` function to extract parts of the date or format it. Format the `scheduled_departure` column to show only the year and month (e.g. '%Y-%m'). Return the formatted string and the count of flights scheduled for each month. Name the columns `flight_month` and `total_flights`, and group by the formatted month string.
solution: SELECT strftime('%Y-%m', scheduled_departure) AS flight_month, COUNT(*) AS total_flights FROM flights GROUP BY flight_month;

title: Update Aircraft Status
description: An aircraft, 'AC001', has just completed maintenance and is now ready for service. The maintenance team needs to update its status in the system from 'Maintenance' to 'Active'. Update the status of aircraft 'AC001' to 'Active'.
solution: UPDATE aircraft SET status = 'Active' WHERE aircraft_id = 'AC001';
verifyQuery: SELECT status FROM aircraft WHERE aircraft_id = 'AC001';

title: Insert a New Flight
description: The scheduling department has finalized a new flight, 'FL005', from 'LAX' to 'SFO', using aircraft 'AC003', scheduled to depart on '2024-06-10 08:00:00' and arrive on '2024-06-10 09:30:00'. Add this new flight to the system.
solution: INSERT INTO flights (flight_id, aircraft_id, departure_airport_id, arrival_airport_id, scheduled_departure, scheduled_arrival) VALUES ('FL005', 'AC003', 'LAX', 'SFO', '2024-06-10 08:00:00', '2024-06-10 09:30:00');

title: Delete a Crew Assignment
description: Due to a last-minute change, employee 'EMP007' has been reassigned from flight 'FL001'. The crew scheduling system needs to reflect this change by removing their assignment. Delete the crew assignment for employee 'EMP007' on flight 'FL001'.
solution: DELETE FROM crew_assignments WHERE flight_id = 'FL001' AND employee_id = 'EMP007';
verifyQuery: SELECT * FROM crew_assignments WHERE flight_id = 'FL001' AND employee_id = 'EMP007';

title: Update Flight Scheduled Arrival
description: Flight 'FL002' has been re-routed due to weather, and its scheduled arrival time has changed. The operations center needs to update the new arrival time to '2024-06-05 18:30:00'. Update the scheduled arrival time for flight 'FL002'.
solution: UPDATE flights SET scheduled_arrival = '2024-06-05 18:30:00' WHERE flight_id = 'FL002';
verifyQuery: SELECT scheduled_arrival FROM flights WHERE flight_id = 'FL002';

title: Insert a New Airport
description: A new regional airport, 'RGN', named 'Regional Gateway', located in 'Springfield', 'USA', with timezone 'America/Chicago', has opened and needs to be added to the system. Add this new airport to the `airports` table.
solution: INSERT INTO airports (airport_id, name, city, country, timezone) VALUES ('RGN', 'Regional Gateway', 'Springfield', 'USA', 'America/Chicago');

title: Add New Flight Status Entry
description: Flight 'FL003' has just landed. The ground crew needs to record its 'Landed' status with the current timestamp. Add a new status entry for flight 'FL003' with status 'Landed' and a timestamp of '2024-06-06 14:15:00'. (Assume a unique `status_id` like 'ST005').
solution: INSERT INTO flight_status_history (status_id, flight_id, status, timestamp) VALUES ('ST005', 'FL003', 'Landed', '2024-06-06 14:15:00');

title: Swap Aircraft Model Due to Maintenance
description: Aircraft 'AC002' is undergoing unexpected maintenance, and its model needs to be temporarily updated to 'Airbus A320' to reflect a replacement aircraft. Update the model of aircraft 'AC002' to 'Airbus A320'.
solution: UPDATE aircraft SET model = 'Airbus A320' WHERE aircraft_id = 'AC002';
verifyQuery: SELECT model FROM aircraft WHERE aircraft_id = 'AC002';

title: Delete Old Flights
description: To keep the database lean and relevant, the system administrator wants to archive or delete flights that occurred before '2024-01-01'. Delete all flights where the scheduled departure is before '2024-01-01'.
solution: DELETE FROM flights WHERE scheduled_departure < '2024-01-01';
verifyQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM flights WHERE scheduled_departure < '2024-01-01';

title: Add 'is_international' Column to Airports
description: The route planning department needs to easily distinguish between international and domestic airports. Add a new column named `is_international` of type BOOLEAN (or INTEGER for SQLite) to the `airports` table, with a default value of 0 (false).
solution: ALTER TABLE airports ADD COLUMN is_international INTEGER DEFAULT 0;

title: Rename 'status' Column in Aircraft
description: To improve clarity, the fleet management team wants to rename the `status` column in the `aircraft` table to `operational_status`. Rename the column accordingly.
solution: ALTER TABLE aircraft RENAME COLUMN status TO operational_status;

title: Add and Drop a Dummy Column from Crew Assignments
description: During a database refactoring, a temporary column `temp_notes` was added to `crew_assignments` for a specific task. Now that the task is complete, this column needs to be removed. First, add `temp_notes` TEXT, then drop it.
solution:
ALTER TABLE crew_assignments ADD COLUMN temp_notes TEXT;
ALTER TABLE crew_assignments DROP COLUMN temp_notes;

title: Create 'maintenance_logs' Table
description: The maintenance department needs a dedicated table to log all maintenance activities for aircraft. Create a new table named `maintenance_logs` with columns: `log_id` (TEXT PRIMARY KEY), `aircraft_id` (TEXT, FOREIGN KEY to `aircraft`), `description` (TEXT), `log_date` (TEXT), and `cost` (REAL).
solution:
CREATE TABLE maintenance_logs (
  log_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  aircraft_id TEXT,
  description TEXT,
  log_date TEXT,
  cost REAL,
  FOREIGN KEY (aircraft_id) REFERENCES aircraft(aircraft_id)
);
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Add CHECK Constraint to Aircraft Capacity
description: To ensure data integrity, the `capacity` of an aircraft must always be a positive number. Add a `CHECK` constraint to the `aircraft` table to enforce that `capacity` is greater than 0.

title: Modify 'status' Column Type in Aircraft
description: The `status` column in the `aircraft` table needs to be more explicitly defined for future compatibility, even though SQLite is flexible. Change its type to `VARCHAR(50)`. (Note: SQLite doesn't enforce length, but the intent is to define it).

title: Add NOT NULL Constraint to Aircraft Model
description: Every aircraft must have a model specified. Add a `NOT NULL` constraint to the `model` column in the `aircraft` table to prevent null values.

title: Rename 'flight_status_history' Table
description: The database administrator wants to rename the `flight_status_history` table to `flight_logs` for better consistency with other logging tables. Rename the table accordingly.
solution: ALTER TABLE flight_status_history RENAME TO flight_logs;
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='flight_logs';

title: Airline Routes
description: Establish the database layout for airlines and their flight routes. Write the code for an `airlines` table with `airline_id` configured as an in-line INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, plus a `name` column (TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE). Also, draft a `routes` table with `route_id` (INTEGER), `origin` (TEXT NOT NULL), `destination` (TEXT NOT NULL), `distance` (INTEGER), and `airline_id` (INTEGER). Apply out-of-line constraints assigning `route_id` as the PRIMARY KEY and `airline_id` as a FOREIGN KEY that references `airlines(airline_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE airlines (airline_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE); CREATE TABLE routes (route_id INTEGER, origin TEXT NOT NULL, destination TEXT NOT NULL, distance INTEGER, airline_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (route_id), FOREIGN KEY (airline_id) REFERENCES airlines(airline_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Airport Terminals
description: Establish a system for airports and their terminals. Set up an `airports_hub` table with an in-line `hub_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY), `code` (TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE), and `city` (TEXT). Follow this by creating a `terminals` table carrying `terminal_id` (INTEGER), `gate_count` (INTEGER), `is_active` (INTEGER DEFAULT 1), and `hub_id` (INTEGER). For the `terminals` table, declare out-of-line conditions to make `terminal_id` the PRIMARY KEY, a CHECK that `is_active IN (0, 1)`, and a FOREIGN KEY mapping `hub_id` to `airports_hub(hub_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE airports_hub (hub_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, code TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, city TEXT); CREATE TABLE terminals (terminal_id INTEGER, gate_count INTEGER, is_active INTEGER DEFAULT 1, hub_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (terminal_id), CHECK (is_active IN (0, 1)), FOREIGN KEY (hub_id) REFERENCES airports_hub(hub_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Fleet Maintenance Logs
description: Structure a database for aircraft repairs. Set up a `parts_catalog` table using an in-line `part_no` (VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY) and `description` (VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL). Draft a `repair_logs` table equipped with `log_id` (BIGINT), `hours_logged` (DECIMAL(5,1) NOT NULL), `repair_date` (DATE), and `part_no` (VARCHAR(50)). Supply out-of-line constraints in `repair_logs`: a PRIMARY KEY for `log_id`, a CHECK ensuring `hours_logged > 0.0`, and a FOREIGN KEY pointing `part_no` to `parts_catalog(part_no)`. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE parts_catalog (part_no VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY, description VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE repair_logs (log_id BIGINT, hours_logged DECIMAL(5,1) NOT NULL, repair_date DATE, part_no VARCHAR(50), PRIMARY KEY (log_id), CHECK (hours_logged > 0.0), FOREIGN KEY (part_no) REFERENCES parts_catalog(part_no));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Flights with Significant Delays
description: The customer service department needs to identify flights that were significantly delayed upon arrival. A flight is considered significantly delayed if its latest 'Landed' status timestamp is more than 60 minutes after its `scheduled_arrival`. Retrieve the flight ID, scheduled arrival, and actual landed timestamp for such flights. Name the result column 'actual_landed_time'.
solution: SELECT f.flight_id, f.scheduled_arrival, fsh.timestamp AS actual_landed_time FROM flights f JOIN flight_status_history fsh ON f.flight_id = fsh.flight_id WHERE fsh.status = 'Landed' AND fsh.timestamp = (SELECT MAX(timestamp) FROM flight_status_history WHERE flight_id = f.flight_id AND status = 'Landed') AND (julianday(fsh.timestamp) - julianday(f.scheduled_arrival)) * 24 * 60 > 60;

title: Upcoming Flights from an Airport in Next 24 Hours
description: The operations team at 'LAX' airport needs to see all flights scheduled to depart from their airport within the next 24 hours from '2024-06-01 10:00:00'. This helps in short-term resource planning. Retrieve the flight ID, scheduled departure, and arrival airport for these flights.
solution: SELECT flight_id, scheduled_departure, arrival_airport_id FROM flights WHERE departure_airport_id = 'LAX' AND scheduled_departure > '2024-06-01 10:00:00' AND scheduled_departure <= DATETIME('2024-06-01 10:00:00', '+1 day');

title: Grounded Aircraft Not Assigned to Upcoming Flights
description: The maintenance and scheduling departments need to identify aircraft that are currently 'Grounded' and are not assigned to any future flights. This helps in planning maintenance or reassigning them. Retrieve the aircraft ID and model of such aircraft.
solution: SELECT aircraft_id, model FROM aircraft WHERE status = 'Grounded' AND aircraft_id NOT IN (SELECT aircraft_id FROM flights WHERE scheduled_departure > DATETIME('now'));

title: Airports with No Departing Flights
description: The network planning team wants to identify airports that currently have no scheduled departing flights. This could indicate underutilized infrastructure or potential route cancellations. Retrieve the name and city of airports with no departing flights.
solution: SELECT name, city FROM airports WHERE airport_id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT departure_airport_id FROM flights);

title: Flights with Multiple Status Changes
description: Flight supervisors want to monitor flights that have experienced an unusually high number of status changes, as this might indicate operational issues. Identify flights that have more than 3 entries in their `flight_status_history`. Retrieve the flight ID and the count of status changes. Name the result column 'status_change_count'.
solution: SELECT flight_id, COUNT(status_id) AS status_change_count FROM flight_status_history GROUP BY flight_id HAVING COUNT(status_id) > 3;

title: Busiest Airport by Total Flights
description: The airport management team wants to determine the busiest airport based on the total number of departures and arrivals. This metric helps in understanding overall traffic. Retrieve the airport ID and its total flight count (departures + arrivals). Name the result column 'total_flights'.
solution: SELECT airport_id, SUM(flight_count) AS total_flights FROM (SELECT departure_airport_id AS airport_id, COUNT(flight_id) AS flight_count FROM flights GROUP BY departure_airport_id UNION ALL SELECT arrival_airport_id AS airport_id, COUNT(flight_id) AS flight_count FROM flights GROUP BY arrival_airport_id) GROUP BY airport_id ORDER BY total_flights DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Crew Members Working on Flights to/from a Specific City
description: The HR department needs to identify all crew members (by employee ID) who have worked on flights that either departed from or arrived in 'London'. This helps in understanding crew exposure to specific routes. Retrieve the distinct employee IDs of these crew members.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT ca.employee_id FROM crew_assignments ca JOIN flights f ON ca.flight_id = f.flight_id JOIN airports da ON f.departure_airport_id = da.airport_id JOIN airports aa ON f.arrival_airport_id = aa.airport_id WHERE da.city = 'London' OR aa.city = 'London';

title: Aircraft Models Never Assigned to a Flight
description: The fleet planning department wants to identify any aircraft models that exist in the fleet but have never been assigned to a flight. This could indicate unused assets or data entry errors. Retrieve the model of such aircraft.
solution: SELECT model FROM aircraft WHERE aircraft_id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT aircraft_id FROM flights);

title: Flights Within the Same Country
description: The domestic operations team needs to identify all flights where both the departure and arrival airports are located within the same country. This helps in analyzing domestic route efficiency. Retrieve the flight ID, departure airport ID, and arrival airport ID for these flights.
solution: SELECT f.flight_id, f.departure_airport_id, f.arrival_airport_id FROM flights f JOIN airports da ON f.departure_airport_id = da.airport_id JOIN airports aa ON f.arrival_airport_id = aa.airport_id WHERE da.country = aa.country;

title: Select Basics
description: Our marketing department is gearing up for a major product launch next quarter and needs to understand our customer base better. To effectively segment users for targeted email campaigns, they require a comprehensive dataset of all registered members. A marketing campaign requires a list of all current users indicating their first and last names. Retrieve every column from the users table so the team has full context on our registered members.
solution: SELECT * FROM users;

title: Filter by Price
description: The sales team is looking for high-ticket items to promote in the upcoming holiday sale. Find all products where the price is strictly greater than 100 to filter out budget items.
solution: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 100;

title: ORDER BY
description: The pricing team is reviewing our low-cost inventory. Find all products ordered by their price in ascending order (lowest to highest) so they can decide what to discount next.
solution: SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY price ASC;

title: Filter with LIKE
description: A user forgot their password but only remembers that their email ends with '@example.com'. Find all users whose email matches this pattern using the LIKE operator.
solution: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%@example.com';

title: Select Distinct
description: The fulfillment center wants a unique list of products that need to be packaged today. Select distinct product_ordered from the orders table so they know which bins to pull from without duplicates.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT product_ordered FROM orders;

title: Filter with IN
description: The warehouse is moving bins for products p1 and p3. Find all information regarding products with the ID 'p1' or 'p3' using the IN operator to update their location tags.
solution: SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_id IN ('p1', 'p3');

title: Filter with BETWEEN
description: Marketing is building a 'Mid-Tier Gifts' landing page. Find products with a price starting from 50 up to 500 so they can populate the new webpage section.
solution: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price BETWEEN 50 AND 500;

title: Limit Results
description: The executive dashboard only has room to display the top 2 premium items. Return only the top 2 most expensive products by ordering by price descending and limiting the results.
solution: SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 2;

title: Wildcard Search
description: We are sending a personalized welcome email testing out names. Find users whose first name starts with 'J' to form a pilot test group for the campaign.
solution: SELECT * FROM users WHERE first_name LIKE 'J%';

title: Multicolumn Sort
description: The HR department requested a printed directory of all users. Order the users by last_name alphabetically (ASC), and then by first_name alphabetically (ASC) to make it easy to read.
solution: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY last_name ASC, first_name ASC;

title: Select Specific Columns
description: The newsletter service strictly demands only the user's name and email. Select only the first_name and email columns from the users table, avoiding SELECT * to save bandwidth.
solution: SELECT first_name, email FROM users;

title: Filter with AND
description: Our marketing team is preparing for the launch of a new 'Premium but Affordable' collection, aiming to capture customers who seek quality products without an exorbitant price tag. To curate this exclusive catalog section, we need to identify all products that fit within a specific, attractive price bracket. The 'Premium but Affordable' collection needs population. Find products that cost strictly more than 100 AND strictly less than 800 to feature in the upcoming catalog.
solution: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 100 AND price < 800;

title: Filter with OR
description: Our marketing team is preparing to ship prizes to the lucky winners of our latest customer loyalty contest. We need to quickly identify the records for Alice and Bob to ensure their prizes are dispatched correctly. Find users with the first_name 'Alice' OR 'Bob' so we can verify their addresses.
solution: SELECT * FROM users WHERE first_name = 'Alice' OR first_name = 'Bob';

title: Inner Join
description: In the fast-paced world of e-commerce, customer satisfaction is paramount. When a user contacts support with a question or issue regarding a past purchase, the support agent needs to quickly and accurately identify the specific product the customer is referring to. Customer support needs to find out what product a specific user ordered. Join the users table and orders table to return the user's first name along with the product they ordered.
solution: SELECT users.first_name, orders.product_ordered FROM users INNER JOIN orders ON users.user_id = orders.user;

title: Left Join
description: Your e-commerce company is launching a new re-engagement campaign. To effectively target potential customers and understand overall user activity, the marketing team needs a comprehensive list of all registered users, highlighting who has made a purchase and who hasn't yet. We want to see all users, and if they have placed an order, see their order ID. Use a LEFT JOIN from users to orders to make sure we don't drop users who haven't bought anything yet (they are potential leads).
solution: SELECT users.first_name, orders.order_id FROM users LEFT JOIN orders ON users.user_id = orders.user;

title: Aggregate: COUNT
description: Our product managers are constantly evaluating the breadth of our offerings to ensure we're meeting market demand and optimizing our portfolio. Knowing the total number of unique products is a fundamental metric for strategic planning and resource allocation. The business analytics team needs a top-line metric. Count the total number of products we currently have in our catalog to include in the quarterly report.
solution: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products;

title: Aggregate: SUM
description: Our CEO is preparing for the quarterly investor call and needs an immediate snapshot of our company's financial health. A key metric for this is our total gross revenue, which directly reflects our sales performance. Finance is calculating gross revenue. Find the total sum of all orders (total_paid) ever placed in the system to report our overall earnings.
solution: SELECT SUM(total_paid) FROM orders;

title: Aggregate: AVG & MAX
description: As a product manager, understanding market dynamics and pricing strategies is key to success. We need a quick overview of our product catalog's pricing to inform our next sales campaign and inventory decisions. What are the highest and average prices among all products? Return the MAX and ROUNDed AVG (to 2 decimal places) as max_price and avg_price.
solution: SELECT MAX(price) as max_price, ROUND(AVG(price), 2) as avg_price FROM products;

title: GROUP BY
description: We are launching a loyalty program and need to know how many orders each user has placed. Group by user and return the user ID along with the count of their orders.
solution: SELECT user, COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY user;

title: Complex Joins
description: Our e-commerce platform is growing, and to ensure top-notch customer service and accurate financial reconciliation, we frequently need to generate detailed receipts for our customers. This requires linking customer profiles with their specific orders and the products they purchased. We need a detailed invoice report. Join the orders, users, and products tables to return the user's first name, the product's name, and the total they paid. This will be used to generate receipts.
solution: SELECT users.first_name, products.product_name, orders.total_paid FROM orders INNER JOIN users ON orders.user = users.user_id INNER JOIN products ON orders.product_ordered = products.product_id;

title: GROUP BY with HAVING
description: We are searching for 'VIP' customers. Find users who have spent more than 100 in total across all their orders. Group by user and use HAVING to filter the sum, so we can send them a thank you gift. Return the user and the sum of total_paid.
solution: SELECT user, SUM(total_paid) FROM orders GROUP BY user HAVING SUM(total_paid) > 100;

title: Filter Aggregations (COUNT)
description: We want to reward loyal customers who purchase frequently. Find users who have placed more than 1 order. Group by user and use HAVING to check count of order_id > 1 to send them a coupon code.
solution: SELECT user, COUNT(order_id) FROM orders GROUP BY user HAVING COUNT(order_id) > 1;

title: COUNT DISTINCT
description: How many unique individuals have transacted with us? Count the number of DISTINCT users who have placed an order to understand our exact active buyer count.
solution: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user) FROM orders;

title: Find Missing Data (IS NULL)
description: We want to re-engage registered users who haven't bought anything yet. Use a LEFT JOIN to find users who have not placed any orders by checking if orders.order_id IS NULL.
solution: SELECT users.first_name FROM users LEFT JOIN orders ON users.user_id = orders.user WHERE orders.order_id IS NULL;

title: DML: INSERT
description:
Our e-commerce platform is constantly expanding its product catalog to offer the latest gadgets to our customers. A new shipment of innovative tech has just landed, and it's crucial to get these items listed online quickly so they can start generating sales.

A new product has arrived in the warehouse and needs to be added to our online store. Insert the new product with product_id 'p11', product_name 'Wireless Charger', description 'Fast charging pad', and price 40.
solution: INSERT INTO products (product_id, product_name, description, price) VALUES ('p11', 'Wireless Charger', 'Fast charging pad', 40);

title: DML: UPDATE
description: As a key member of the e-commerce operations team, you're responsible for maintaining accurate product data. Recent disruptions in the global supply chain have significantly impacted the cost of our most popular electronics, requiring immediate price adjustments to maintain profitability. Due to a supply chain issue, the price of our top-selling laptop needs to be updated. Update the price of the product with product_name 'Laptop' to 1000.
solution: UPDATE products SET price = 1000 WHERE product_name = 'Laptop';
verifyQuery: SELECT product_name, price FROM products WHERE product_name = 'Laptop';

title: DML: DELETE
description: In the fast-paced world of e-commerce, maintaining data integrity is paramount. Our fraud detection system just flagged an order as suspicious, and it's crucial to remove all traces of it immediately to prevent further issues. Delete the order with order_id 'o2' from the orders table completely.
solution: DELETE FROM orders WHERE order_id = 'o2';

title: Update with Math
description: The finance department has just released its quarterly report, highlighting the impact of recent market inflation on our cost of goods sold. To ensure the company remains profitable and competitive, a universal price adjustment for all products is urgently required. Due to inflation, the company decided to increase the price of all products across the board by 10% (multiply by 1.1). Note: SQLite integer math may drop decimals, which is acceptable for this bulk update.
solution: UPDATE products SET price = price * 1.1;

title: Insert Multiple Rows
description: We received a new shipment containing two new tech accessories. Insert two products at once: ('p12', 'Ethernet Cable', 'Cat6 10ft', 15) and ('p13', 'HDMI Cable', '8K 6ft', 20) to save time on data entry.
solution: INSERT INTO products (product_id, product_name, description, price) VALUES ('p12', 'Ethernet Cable', 'Cat6 10ft', 15), ('p13', 'HDMI Cable', '8K 6ft', 20);
verifyQuery: SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_id IN ('p12', 'p13');

title: Update Multiple Fields
description: User 'u1' legally changed their name. Update user 'u1' simultaneously to have first_name 'Johnny' and last_name 'Appleseed' to keep their profile current.
solution: UPDATE users SET first_name = 'Johnny', last_name = 'Appleseed' WHERE user_id = 'u1';
verifyQuery: SELECT first_name, last_name FROM users WHERE user_id = 'u1';

title: Conditional Delete
description: The company is discontinuing all budget-tier products to focus on high-end electronics. Delete all products that are cheaper than 150 from the catalog.
solution: DELETE FROM products WHERE price < 150;

title: Partial Record Insert
description: A user partially registered via a quick sign-up form that only asks for their first name. Insert a new user but only provide the user_id ('u11') and first_name ('Evelyn'). Let the other columns remain NULL.
solution: INSERT INTO users (user_id, first_name) VALUES ('u11', 'Evelyn');

title: DDL: CREATE TABLE
description: Our e-commerce platform thrives on customer trust and product quality. To better understand what our users love (or don't love!) about our offerings and to help new customers make informed decisions, the product team is adding a new feature for user reviews. Create a table named 'reviews' with columns: review_id (TEXT), user_id (TEXT), product_id (TEXT), and rating (INTEGER) to store this new data.
solution: CREATE TABLE reviews (review_id TEXT, user_id TEXT, product_id TEXT, rating INTEGER);

title: DDL: ALTER TABLE
description: The marketing team wants to start sending SMS notifications. We need a way to store user phone numbers. Add a new column named 'phone' of type TEXT to the users table.
solution: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone TEXT;

title: CREATE INDEX
description: Our database queries for users by email are getting slow! Create an index named 'idx_user_email' on the 'email' column of the users table to speed up lookup times.
solution: CREATE INDEX idx_user_email ON users(email);
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='index' AND name='idx_user_email';

title: CREATE VIEW
description: The analytics team frequently runs a complex query joining users and orders. To make their lives easier, create a view called 'user_orders' that selects first_name from users and total_paid from orders.
solution: CREATE VIEW user_orders AS SELECT users.first_name, orders.total_paid FROM users INNER JOIN orders ON users.user_id = orders.user;

title: DROP TABLE
description: The new reviews architecture was scrapped, and the table is no longer needed. Keep the database clean by using DROP TABLE to delete the 'reviews' table from the schema completely.
solution: DROP TABLE reviews;

For the `employees` table, use an out-of-line PRIMARY KEY for `emp_id` and an out-of-line FOREIGN KEY for `dept_id` referencing `departments(dept_id)`. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE departments (dept_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, dept_name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE); CREATE TABLE employees (emp_id INTEGER, first_name TEXT NOT NULL, last_name TEXT NOT NULL, salary INTEGER, dept_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (emp_id), FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES departments(dept_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('departments') UNION ALL SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('employees') UNION ALL SELECT "from", "table", 0, 0 FROM pragma_foreign_key_list('employees');

description: Create a `suppliers` table with an in-line `supplier_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY), `name` (TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE), and `contact_email` (TEXT). In the `inventory` table, use an out-of-line PRIMARY KEY for `item_id`, an out-of-line CHECK constraint ensuring `quantity >= 0`, and an out-of-line FOREIGN KEY linking `supplier_id` to `suppliers(supplier_id)`. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE suppliers (supplier_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, contact_email TEXT); CREATE TABLE inventory (item_id INTEGER, item_name TEXT NOT NULL, quantity INTEGER DEFAULT 0, supplier_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (item_id), CHECK (quantity >= 0), FOREIGN KEY (supplier_id) REFERENCES suppliers(supplier_id));

Then, create an `audit_logs` table with `log_id` (BIGINT), `action_date` (TIMESTAMP NOT NULL), `currency_code` (VARCHAR(3)), and `amount` (DECIMAL(15,2)). Use out-of-line constraints in `audit_logs`: PRIMARY KEY for `log_id`, a CHECK constraint ensuring `amount > 0`, and a FOREIGN KEY linking `currency_code` to `currencies(currency_code)`. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE currencies (currency_code VARCHAR(3) PRIMARY KEY, exchange_rate DECIMAL(10,4) NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE audit_logs (log_id BIGINT, action_date TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, currency_code VARCHAR(3), amount DECIMAL(15,2), PRIMARY KEY (log_id), CHECK (amount > 0), FOREIGN KEY (currency_code) REFERENCES currencies(currency_code));
