title: Custom Client View
description: Create a view named `client_summary` that displays only two columns from the `clients` table: `contact_email` and `client_name`, in that exact order.
solution: CREATE VIEW client_summary AS SELECT contact_email, client_name FROM clients;

title: Detailed Client View & Alteration
description: First, create a view named `detailed_view` containing `client_name`, `contact_email`, and `phone_number` in that order from the `clients` table. Then, since SQLite does not support ALTER VIEW, drop `detailed_view` and recreate it pulling the columns in the opposite order: `phone_number`, `contact_email`, `client_name`. Separate your statements with semicolons.
solution: CREATE VIEW detailed_view AS SELECT client_name, contact_email, phone_number FROM clients; DROP VIEW detailed_view; CREATE VIEW detailed_view AS SELECT phone_number, contact_email, client_name FROM clients;

title: Top Spending User
description: Identify our absolute best customer for a PR campaign. Find the first_name and last_name of the user who has spent the most money in total. You will need a JOIN, GROUP BY, ORDER BY SUM, and LIMIT 1.
solution: SELECT users.first_name, users.last_name FROM users INNER JOIN orders ON users.user_id = orders.user GROUP BY users.user_id ORDER BY SUM(orders.total_paid) DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Products Never Ordered
description: We have dead stock sitting in the warehouse. Find the product_name for products that have never been ordered using a LEFT JOIN from products to orders, checking for IS NULL.
solution: SELECT products.product_name FROM products LEFT JOIN orders ON products.product_id = orders.product_ordered WHERE orders.order_id IS NULL;

title: Above Average Price
description: We want to analyze our premium tier offerings. Find the product_name and price of products whose price is strictly greater than the overall average price of all products using a subquery.
solution: SELECT product_name, price FROM products WHERE price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM products);

title: Average Order Value For Repeat Customers
description: What is the lifetime value of returning customers? Find the average order value (total_paid) per user, but ONLY for users who have placed more than 1 order. Return the first_name and average total_paid rounded to 2 decimal places as avg_paid.
solution: SELECT users.first_name, ROUND(AVG(orders.total_paid), 2) as avg_paid FROM users INNER JOIN orders ON users.user_id = orders.user GROUP BY users.user_id HAVING COUNT(orders.order_id) > 1;

title: All Students
description: The academic advising office is preparing for the upcoming semester and needs a quick overview of all students' academic standing to identify those who might need support or special recognition. We need a general roster with key academic performance indicators. Retrieve the first name, last name, and GPA of all students.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name, gpa FROM students;

title: High Achievers
description: The Dean's office is compiling the list for academic honors and scholarships for the current semester. To recognize our top-performing students, we need to identify those who have achieved exceptional academic results. Find the first and last names of students with a GPA greater than 3.5.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name FROM students WHERE gpa > 3.5;

title: 4-Credit Courses
description: Academic advisors are assisting students with course registration and need to quickly identify courses that satisfy a common 4-credit requirement. Write a query to find the names of all courses that are worth 4 credits.
solution: SELECT course_name FROM courses WHERE credits = 4;

title: Recent Enrollments
description: The alumni relations office is planning a special networking event for recent cohorts and needs to identify eligible students. Write a query to find the first and last names of students who enrolled in or after 2022, ordered by their enrollment year descending.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name FROM students WHERE enrollment_year >= 2022 ORDER BY enrollment_year DESC;

title: Top Students
description: The scholarship committee is in the final stages of reviewing applications and needs to identify the absolute highest-performing students for prestigious awards. Retrieve the first name, last name, and GPA of the top 3 students based on their GPA, in descending order.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name, gpa FROM students ORDER BY gpa DESC LIMIT 3;

title: Low GPA Alert
description: Academic advisors are preparing for mid-semester check-ins and need to identify students who might be struggling academically to offer support. Retrieve the student IDs and GPAs of students who have a GPA strictly less than 3.0.
solution: SELECT student_id, gpa FROM students WHERE gpa < 3.0;

title: All Enrollments
description: For administrative purposes, such as auditing student records or generating reports, it's essential to have a structured view of all course enrollments. This ordered list helps in quickly locating specific student-course pairings. Write a query to retrieve all fields from the enrollments table ordered by student_id then by course_id.
solution: SELECT * FROM enrollments ORDER BY student_id ASC, course_id ASC;

title: Select Specific Grades
description: The Dean's office wants to recognize high-achieving students and analyze their course selections. Identifying all 'A' grades helps in compiling honor rolls and understanding academic excellence. Find the student_id and course_id of all enrollments where the grade is 'A'.
solution: SELECT student_id, course_id FROM enrollments WHERE grade = 'A';

title: Student Enrollments
description: The registrar's office is processing transcript requests and needs to generate a comprehensive view of each student's academic performance across all their enrolled courses. To accurately display their grades, we need to connect student records with their specific course enrollments. Join students and enrollments to retrieve the first_name, last_name, and the grade they received in their courses.
solution: SELECT s.first_name, s.last_name, e.grade FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id;

title: Professor Departments
description: The university administration is updating its internal faculty directory and needs to ensure that every professor is correctly associated with their respective academic department. This information is crucial for departmental budgeting and communication. For every professor, list their last_name and the department_name they belong to.
solution: SELECT p.last_name, d.department_name FROM professors p JOIN departments d ON p.department_id = d.department_id;

title: Class of 2022 Average GPA
description: The university administration is conducting an annual review of student performance and wants to assess the academic standing of the most recent incoming class. Calculate the average GPA of students who enrolled in the year 2022. Return the enrollment_year and the result as avg_gpa rounded to 1 decimal place.
solution: SELECT enrollment_year, ROUND(AVG(gpa), 1) as avg_gpa FROM students WHERE enrollment_year = 2022 GROUP BY enrollment_year;

title: Courses per Department
description: List each department's name along with the number of courses they offer. Join departments with courses. Select department_name and count the courses as course_count.
solution: SELECT d.department_name, COUNT(c.course_id) as course_count FROM departments d LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.department_id = c.department_id GROUP BY d.department_name;

title: Grades in Calculus I
description: The mathematics department is conducting an internal review of student success in foundational courses to identify potential areas for curriculum enhancement. Find the grades received by students in the 'Calculus I' course. Return the student's first name, last name, and their grade.
solution: SELECT s.first_name, s.last_name, e.grade FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.course_id WHERE c.course_name = 'Calculus I';

title: Average GPA by Year
description: Calculate the average GPA for each enrollment year. Return the enrollment_year and the average GPA as avg_gpa rounded to 1 decimal place, ordered by enrollment year.
solution: SELECT enrollment_year, ROUND(AVG(gpa), 1) as avg_gpa FROM students GROUP BY enrollment_year ORDER BY enrollment_year;

title: Total Credits per Department
description: The academic planning office is preparing its annual report and needs a clear overview of the total academic load and resource allocation for each department. Find the total number of credits offered by each department. Return department_name and the sum of credits as total_credits. (Only include departments that offer at least one course).
solution: SELECT d.department_name, SUM(c.credits) as total_credits FROM departments d JOIN courses c ON d.department_id = c.department_id GROUP BY d.department_name;

title: Einstein's Students
description: A special departmental event is being organized, and we need to identify all students who are part of the same academic community as Professor Einstein. Retrieve the first name and last name of students enrolled in courses offered by the department Albert Einstein belongs to.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT s.first_name, s.last_name FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.course_id JOIN professors p ON c.department_id = p.department_id WHERE p.first_name = 'Albert' AND p.last_name = 'Einstein';

title: Min and Max GPA
description: To understand the overall academic performance range within the student body, the administration needs to know the extreme GPA values. This helps in setting benchmarks and identifying outliers. Find the lowest and highest GPA among all students. Return them as min_gpa and max_gpa.
solution: SELECT MIN(gpa) as min_gpa, MAX(gpa) as max_gpa FROM students;

title: Count of Grades
description: The academic department wants to analyze the distribution of grades across all courses to assess grading consistency and student performance trends. This provides a quick overview of how many students received each particular grade. Group the enrollments by grade and count how many enrollments fall into each category. Return grade and the count as grade_count.
solution: SELECT grade, COUNT(*) as grade_count FROM enrollments GROUP BY grade;

title: Courses By Department
description: Find out how many courses are offered by each department. Join departments and courses, grouped by department_name. Return department_name and course_count. (Only include departments that offer at least one course).
solution: SELECT d.department_name, COUNT(c.course_id) as course_count FROM departments d JOIN courses c ON d.department_id = c.department_id GROUP BY d.department_name;

title: Professors With Multiple Courses
description: To understand departmental activity and resource allocation, we need to identify professors associated with departments that are actively offering a diverse range of courses. This helps in evaluating departmental breadth. Find the first_name and last_name of professors who belong to a department that offers more than 1 course.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT p.first_name, p.last_name FROM professors p JOIN courses c ON p.department_id = c.department_id GROUP BY p.professor_id, p.first_name, p.last_name HAVING COUNT(c.course_id) > 1;

title: New Enrollment
description: Insert a new student into the students table. Use student_id 'st7', first_name 'Gina', last_name 'Linetti', enrollment_year 2024, and gpa 4.0.
solution: INSERT INTO students (student_id, first_name, last_name, enrollment_year, gpa) VALUES ('st7', 'Gina', 'Linetti', 2024, 4.0);

title: Update Evan's GPA
description: Update the gpa to 3.0 for the student with student_id 'st5'.
solution: UPDATE students SET gpa = 3.0 WHERE student_id = 'st5';

title: Remove Dropped Course
description: A course was dropped due to low enrollment. Delete the course named 'Linear Algebra' from the courses table.
solution: DELETE FROM courses WHERE course_name = 'Linear Algebra';

title: Update Professor Department
description: Grace Hopper has moved to the 'd2' department. Update her department_id.
solution: UPDATE professors SET department_id = 'd2' WHERE first_name = 'Grace' AND last_name = 'Hopper';

title: Clear Old Enrollments
description: Clear all old enrollments for the student with student_id 'st2'.
solution: DELETE FROM enrollments WHERE student_id = 'st2';

title: Reassign Students
description: Student 'st2' is dropping a specific course and 'st1' is taking over their spot! Actually, let's just make 'st1' take over all 'st2's 'C' grade classes (just imagine). Wait: Update the student_id of enrollments from 'st2' to 'st1'. Then SELECT all enrollments for 'st1'.
solution: UPDATE enrollments SET student_id = 'st1' WHERE student_id = 'st2';

title: Remove Intro Courses
description: Our curriculum is undergoing a major restructuring, and all generic 'Introduction' courses are being replaced with more specialized foundational courses. We need to remove the old course entries from the system. Delete all courses that have 'Introduction' anywhere in their name. Then return all remaining courses.
solution: DELETE FROM courses WHERE course_name LIKE '%Introduction%';

title: Create Clubs Table
description: The student affairs office is launching a new initiative to better organize and promote the vibrant student club scene on campus. Create a new table named 'clubs' with columns 'club_id' (TEXT PRIMARY KEY) and 'club_name' (TEXT).
solution: CREATE TABLE clubs (club_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, club_name TEXT);

title: Add Student Email
description: To enhance communication and streamline official notifications, the university registrar's office needs to directly store student email addresses within the main student records system. Add a new column called 'email' of type TEXT to the students table.
solution: ALTER TABLE students ADD COLUMN email TEXT;

title: Drop Clubs Table
description: The university has decided to streamline its student activity tracking, and the old 'clubs' database table is now obsolete. Remove it from the schema.
solution: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS clubs;
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Create Assignments Table
description: To better manage student coursework and grading, the academic department needs a dedicated place to store assignment details. Create a new table named 'assignments' with columns 'assignment_id' (TEXT PRIMARY KEY), 'course_id' (TEXT), and 'max_score' (INTEGER).
solution: CREATE TABLE assignments (assignment_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, course_id TEXT, max_score INTEGER);

title: Drop Assignments Table
description: The university is transitioning to a new learning management system, Canvas, which will handle all assignment submissions and grading. The old 'assignments' table in our local database is now obsolete and needs to be removed. We are moving assignments to Canvas instead. Drop the 'assignments' table if it exists.
solution: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS assignments;
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Create Staff Table
description: To better manage our non-faculty personnel and their roles within the institution, we need a dedicated table for staff information. This will help in tracking administrative and support staff details. Create a new table named 'staff' with columns: 'staff_id' (TEXT PRIMARY KEY), 'name' (TEXT), and 'role' (TEXT).
solution: CREATE TABLE staff (staff_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, role TEXT);

title: Academic Departments
description: Design a schema for academic departments. First, create a 'departments_new' table with 'dept_id' (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY) and 'dept_name' (TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE). Then, create a 'courses_new' table with 'course_id' (INTEGER), 'title' (TEXT NOT NULL), 'credits' (INTEGER), and 'dept_id' (INTEGER). Apply an out-of-line PRIMARY KEY constraint for 'course_id' and an out-of-line FOREIGN KEY constraint for 'dept_id' referencing 'departments_new(dept_id)'. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE departments_new (dept_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, dept_name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE); CREATE TABLE courses_new (course_id INTEGER, title TEXT NOT NULL, credits INTEGER, dept_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (course_id), FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES departments_new(dept_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Student Enrollments
description: Set up a student enrollment system. First, create a `students` table featuring an in-line `student_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY), `full_name` (TEXT NOT NULL), and `enrollment_year` (INTEGER). Next, create an `enrollments` table with `enrollment_id` (INTEGER), `grade` (TEXT), `student_id` (INTEGER), and `course_id` (INTEGER NOT NULL). For `enrollments`, configure out-of-line constraints: a PRIMARY KEY on `enrollment_id`, a CHECK constraint ensuring `grade IN ('A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'F')`, and a FOREIGN KEY on `student_id` referencing `students(student_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE students (student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, full_name TEXT NOT NULL, enrollment_year INTEGER); CREATE TABLE enrollments (enrollment_id INTEGER, grade TEXT, student_id INTEGER, course_id INTEGER NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY (enrollment_id), CHECK (grade IN ('A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'F')), FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('students') UNION ALL SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('enrollments') UNION ALL SELECT "from", "table", 0, 0 FROM pragma_foreign_key_list('enrollments');

title: Scholarship Disbursements
description: Set up a scholarship tracking system. Create a `donors` table using an in-line `donor_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY), `full_name` (VARCHAR(150) NOT NULL), and `is_anonymous` (BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE). Following that, create a `disbursements` table with `disbursement_id` (BIGINT), `amount_paid` (DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL), `payout_date` (DATE NOT NULL), and `donor_id` (INTEGER). In `disbursements`, define out-of-line constraints: `disbursement_id` as the PRIMARY KEY, a CHECK that `amount_paid >= 100.00`, and a FOREIGN KEY linking `donor_id` to `donors(donor_id)`. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE donors (donor_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, full_name VARCHAR(150) NOT NULL, is_anonymous BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE); CREATE TABLE disbursements (disbursement_id BIGINT, amount_paid DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL, payout_date DATE NOT NULL, donor_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (disbursement_id), CHECK (amount_paid >= 100.00), FOREIGN KEY (donor_id) REFERENCES donors(donor_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('donors') UNION ALL SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('disbursements') UNION ALL SELECT "from", "table", 0, 0 FROM pragma_foreign_key_list('disbursements');

title: Adding Foreign Key via Re-creation
description: You have a `students` table and an unlinked `lockers` table which contains a `student_id` column. Since SQLite does not support adding FOREIGN KEY constraints via ALTER TABLE, you must link them by re-creating `lockers`. Rename `lockers` to `lockers_old`. Create a new `lockers` table with `locker_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY), `student_id` (INTEGER), and a `FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id)`. Copy data from `lockers_old` to the new `lockers`, then drop `lockers_old`.
solution: ALTER TABLE lockers RENAME TO lockers_old; CREATE TABLE lockers (locker_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, student_id INTEGER, FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id)); INSERT INTO lockers SELECT * FROM lockers_old; DROP TABLE lockers_old;

title: Optimizing Student Lookups
description: Queries looking up students by their `last_name` are running slow. Create an index on the `last_name` column of the `students_index` table. Name the index `idx_student_last_name`.
solution: CREATE INDEX idx_student_last_name ON students_index(last_name);

title: Composite Department & Course Index
description: We often filter courses by both `department_id` and `credits`. Create a composite index named `idx_dept_credits` on the `courses_index` table using the `department_id` and `credits` columns together.
solution: CREATE INDEX idx_dept_credits ON courses_index(department_id, credits);

title: Alice's Grades in CS
description: As a student advisor, you need to quickly review Alice Smith's performance in a core computer science course to guide her academic path. Find the grade Alice Smith received in 'Introduction to Programming'.
solution: SELECT e.grade FROM enrollments e JOIN students s ON e.student_id = s.student_id JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.course_id WHERE s.first_name = 'Alice' AND s.last_name = 'Smith' AND c.course_name = 'Introduction to Programming';

title: A-Grade Students
description: The Dean's office is preparing for an academic awards ceremony and wants to identify all high-achieving students. Find the distinct first and last names of students who have received at least one 'A' grade in their courses.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT s.first_name, s.last_name FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id WHERE e.grade = 'A';

title: Multi-Course Departments
description: The university's curriculum committee is conducting an audit to ensure a diverse and robust academic catalog across all departments. Retrieve the names of departments that offer more than 1 course.
solution: SELECT d.department_name FROM departments d JOIN courses c ON d.department_id = c.department_id GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name HAVING COUNT(c.course_id) > 1;

title: Professors and Course Counts
description: The Dean's office wants to understand the workload distribution across departments and how many courses each department is currently offering. For each professor, retrieve their last name, department name, and the number of courses offered by their department as available_courses.
solution: SELECT p.last_name, d.department_name, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM courses c WHERE c.department_id = p.department_id) as available_courses FROM professors p JOIN departments d ON p.department_id = d.department_id;

title: Students Taking Physics
description: The Physics department is planning an exclusive seminar and needs a list of all students currently engaged in their courses to send invitations. Find the distinct first and last names of students who are taking at least one course offered by the 'Physics' department.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT s.first_name, s.last_name FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.course_id JOIN departments d ON c.department_id = d.department_id WHERE d.department_name = 'Physics';

title: Courses with No Enrollments
description: The registrar's office is reviewing course offerings for the next semester and wants to identify under-enrolled courses that might need to be cancelled or promoted. Find the names of courses that currently have no students enrolled in them.
solution: SELECT course_name FROM courses c LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.course_id = e.course_id WHERE e.enrollment_id IS NULL;

title: Multi-Course Students
description: The academic advising department wants to identify highly engaged students who are taking a heavier course load. This helps in recognizing dedicated learners or potentially identifying students who might need extra support. Find the first_name and last_name of students who are enrolled in more than 2 distinct courses.
solution: SELECT s.first_name, s.last_name FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id GROUP BY s.student_id, s.first_name, s.last_name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT e.course_id) > 2;

title: Highest GPA by Year
description: The university administration is interested in tracking academic excellence trends over time. Identifying the highest GPA achieved in each enrollment year helps in understanding year-to-year student performance peaks and setting new academic goals. For each enrollment_year, find the maximum GPA. Return enrollment_year and the maximum GPA as max_gpa.
solution: SELECT enrollment_year, MAX(gpa) as max_gpa FROM students GROUP BY enrollment_year;

title: List All Patients
description: A hospital administrator needs a complete roster of all registered patients to ensure data integrity and for general record-keeping. This list should include all available details for each patient. Retrieve all columns for every patient in the system.
solution: SELECT * FROM patients;

title: Find Cardiology Specialists
description: The scheduling department needs to quickly identify all healthcare providers who specialize in Cardiology to assign new patient appointments. They require a list of these specialists. Retrieve the first name, last name, and specialty of all providers whose specialty is 'Cardiology'.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name, specialty FROM providers WHERE specialty = 'Cardiology';

title: Appointments on a Specific Date
description: A clinic manager wants to review the schedule for a particular day to anticipate patient flow and resource allocation. They need to see all appointments booked for that day. Retrieve all details for appointments scheduled on '2023-01-15'.
solution: SELECT * FROM appointments WHERE appointment_date = '2023-01-15';

title: Diagnoses for an Appointment
description: A medical biller is processing claims for a specific patient visit and needs to verify all diagnoses recorded during that appointment. They require a list of these diagnoses. Retrieve the ICD code and description for all diagnoses associated with 'appt_001'.
solution: SELECT icd_code, description FROM diagnoses WHERE appointment_id = 'appt_001';

title: Prescriptions for an Appointment
description: A pharmacist is reviewing a patient's medication history after a recent visit and needs to know all medications prescribed during a specific appointment. They need a list of these prescriptions. Retrieve the medication name and dosage for all prescriptions associated with 'appt_002'.
solution: SELECT medication_name, dosage FROM prescriptions WHERE appointment_id = 'appt_002';

title: Female Patients' Details
description: The patient outreach team is planning a health awareness campaign targeting specific demographics. They need to identify all female patients for this initiative. Retrieve the first name, last name, and date of birth of all patients whose gender is 'Female'.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name, date_of_birth FROM patients WHERE gender = 'Female';

title: Pending Appointments
description: The front desk staff needs to quickly identify all appointments that are currently in 'Pending' status to follow up with patients or providers. This helps manage the daily schedule efficiently. Retrieve all details for appointments with a status of 'Pending'.
solution: SELECT * FROM appointments WHERE status = 'Pending';

title: Providers by Last Name Initial
description: A new intern is trying to familiarize themselves with the medical staff and needs to find providers whose last names start with a specific letter. This helps in quickly locating their records. Retrieve the first name, last name, and specialty of all providers whose last name starts with 'S'.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name, specialty FROM providers WHERE last_name LIKE 'S%';

title: Unique Medications Prescribed
description: The pharmacy department wants to analyze the variety of medications being prescribed across the hospital to manage inventory and identify common drug usage patterns. They need a list of all distinct medications. Retrieve all unique medication names that have been prescribed.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT medication_name FROM prescriptions;

title: Patients with Recorded Date of Birth
description: For compliance and demographic analysis, the records department needs to ensure all patient profiles have a complete date of birth. They want to identify any records where this crucial information might be missing. Retrieve the patient_id, first_name, and last_name for all patients whose date of birth is not NULL.
solution: SELECT patient_id, first_name, last_name FROM patients WHERE date_of_birth IS NOT NULL;

title: Total Number of Patients
description: The hospital management needs to know the total number of registered patients for annual reporting and capacity planning. This is a fundamental metric for understanding the hospital's reach. Count the total number of patients in the system. Name the result column 'total_patients'.
solution: SELECT COUNT(patient_id) AS total_patients FROM patients;

title: Appointments Per Provider
description: The administrative team wants to assess the workload of each provider to ensure fair distribution and identify any over- or under-utilized staff. They need a breakdown of appointments per provider. Count the number of appointments for each provider, displaying their first name, last name, and the total count. Name the result column 'total_appointments'.
solution: SELECT p.first_name, p.last_name, COUNT(a.appointment_id) AS total_appointments FROM providers p LEFT JOIN appointments a ON p.provider_id = a.provider_id GROUP BY p.provider_id, p.first_name, p.last_name ORDER BY total_appointments DESC;

title: Patient with Most Appointments
description: The patient relations department wants to identify their most frequent visitors to potentially offer loyalty programs or understand common health patterns. They need to find the patient who has scheduled the highest number of appointments. Find the first name and last name of the patient who has the most appointments. Name the result column 'num_appointments'.
solution: SELECT p.first_name, p.last_name, COUNT(a.appointment_id) AS num_appointments FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.patient_id = a.patient_id GROUP BY p.patient_id, p.first_name, p.last_name ORDER BY num_appointments DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Patients and Their Appointments
description: For a comprehensive patient record review, a clinician needs to see each patient along with all their scheduled appointments. List the first name and last name of each patient, along with their appointment ID and appointment date. Name the columns 'patient_first_name' and 'patient_last_name'. Order the results by patient_last_name and then appointment_date.
solution: SELECT p.first_name AS patient_first_name, p.last_name AS patient_last_name, a.appointment_id, a.appointment_date FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.patient_id = a.patient_id ORDER BY p.last_name, a.appointment_date;

title: Average Prescriptions Per Appointment
description: The pharmacy management team wants to understand the average number of medications prescribed during a single patient visit. This metric can help in inventory forecasting and resource planning. Calculate the average number of prescriptions per appointment. Name the result column 'avg_prescriptions_per_appointment'.
solution: SELECT CAST(COUNT(pr.prescription_id) AS REAL) / COUNT(DISTINCT pr.appointment_id) AS avg_prescriptions_per_appointment FROM prescriptions pr;

title: Provider Appointment Counts
description: The human resources department needs to generate a report showing how many appointments each provider has handled. This data is crucial for performance reviews and staffing decisions. List each provider's first name, last name, and the total count of their appointments. Name the result column 'total_appointments'.
solution: SELECT pr.first_name, pr.last_name, COUNT(a.appointment_id) AS total_appointments FROM providers pr LEFT JOIN appointments a ON pr.provider_id = a.provider_id GROUP BY pr.provider_id, pr.first_name, pr.last_name ORDER BY total_appointments DESC;

title: Most Common Provider Specialty
description: Hospital management wants to understand the dominant medical specialties within their facility to identify areas of strength or potential need for recruitment. They need to find which specialty has the most providers. Determine the most common specialty among all providers. Name the result column 'num_providers'.
solution: SELECT specialty, COUNT(provider_id) AS num_providers FROM providers GROUP BY specialty ORDER BY num_providers DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Patient-Provider Appointment Details
description: For auditing purposes, trace which doctor saw which patient for every appointment. Retrieve the patient's first name, patient's last name, provider's first name, provider's last name, and the appointment date for all appointments. Name the columns 'patient_first_name', 'patient_last_name', 'provider_first_name', and 'provider_last_name'. Order the results by appointment_date.
solution: SELECT p.first_name AS patient_first_name, p.last_name AS patient_last_name, pr.first_name AS provider_first_name, pr.last_name AS provider_last_name, a.appointment_date FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.patient_id = a.patient_id JOIN providers pr ON a.provider_id = pr.provider_id ORDER BY a.appointment_date;

title: Diagnoses Count Per Appointment
description: The medical coding department needs to understand the complexity of each patient visit by counting the number of diagnoses recorded per appointment. Count how many diagnoses each appointment has, showing the appointment ID and the count. Name the result column 'num_diagnoses' and order the results by this count in descending order.
solution: SELECT appointment_id, COUNT(diagnosis_id) AS num_diagnoses FROM diagnoses GROUP BY appointment_id ORDER BY num_diagnoses DESC;

title: Patients Without Any Appointments
description: The patient retention team wants to identify registered patients who have never scheduled an appointment to reach out and encourage engagement. This helps in proactive patient care. Find the first name and last name of patients who have never had an appointment.
solution: SELECT p.first_name, p.last_name FROM patients p LEFT JOIN appointments a ON p.patient_id = a.patient_id WHERE a.appointment_id IS NULL;

title: Register New Patient
description: A new patient, 'Alice Smith', born on '1990-05-10' and identified as 'Female', has just registered at the clinic. Their information needs to be added to the patient database. Insert this new patient into the 'patients' table with a unique patient_id 'pat_006'.
solution: INSERT INTO patients (patient_id, first_name, last_name, date_of_birth, gender) VALUES ('pat_006', 'Alice', 'Smith', '1990-05-10', 'Female');

title: Update Patient's Last Name
description: A patient, 'John Doe' (patient_id 'pat_001'), recently got married and changed their last name to 'Davis'. The patient's record needs to be updated to reflect this change. Update the last name of patient 'pat_001' to 'Davis'.
solution: UPDATE patients SET last_name = 'Davis' WHERE patient_id = 'pat_001';

title: Cancel an Appointment
description: An appointment with ID 'appt_003' has been cancelled by the patient. The system needs to reflect this change accurately to avoid confusion and free up the slot. Update the status of appointment 'appt_003' to 'Cancelled'.
solution: UPDATE appointments SET status = 'Cancelled' WHERE appointment_id = 'appt_003';

title: Add New Provider
description: Dr. Emily White, a new 'Pediatrics' specialist, has joined the hospital staff. Her details need to be added to the provider database so she can be assigned to appointments. Insert this new provider into the 'providers' table with a unique provider_id 'prov_004'.
solution: INSERT INTO providers (provider_id, first_name, last_name, specialty) VALUES ('prov_004', 'Emily', 'White', 'Pediatrics');

title: Mark Appointment as Completed
description: After a patient's visit, the appointment status needs to be updated to 'Completed' for billing and record-keeping purposes. Appointment 'appt_001' has just finished. Update the status of appointment 'appt_001' to 'Completed'.
solution: UPDATE appointments SET status = 'Completed' WHERE appointment_id = 'appt_001';

title: Record New Diagnosis
description: During appointment 'appt_001', a new diagnosis of 'Hypertension' (ICD code 'I10') was made. This diagnosis needs to be added to the patient's record for accurate medical history. Insert this new diagnosis for appointment 'appt_001' with a unique diagnosis_id 'diag_006'.
solution: INSERT INTO diagnoses (diagnosis_id, appointment_id, icd_code, description) VALUES ('diag_006', 'appt_001', 'I10', 'Hypertension');

title: Update Prescription Dosage
description: A patient's medication dosage for 'Amoxicillin' (prescription_id 'pres_001') needs to be adjusted from '250mg' to '500mg' as per the doctor's instructions. The prescription record must be updated. Update the dosage of prescription 'pres_001' to '500mg'.
solution: UPDATE prescriptions SET dosage = '500mg' WHERE prescription_id = 'pres_001';

title: Delete All Prescriptions for an Appointment
description: Due to a data entry error, all prescriptions associated with appointment 'appt_004' were incorrectly recorded. These erroneous entries need to be removed from the system. Delete all prescriptions linked to appointment 'appt_004'.
solution: DELETE FROM prescriptions WHERE appointment_id = 'appt_004';

title: Schedule New Appointment
description: Patient 'pat_001' needs to schedule a follow-up appointment with provider 'prov_001' for '2023-02-01'. This new appointment needs to be added to the system with a 'Scheduled' status. Insert this new appointment with a unique appointment_id 'appt_006'.
solution: INSERT INTO appointments (appointment_id, patient_id, provider_id, appointment_date, status) VALUES ('appt_006', 'pat_001', 'prov_001', '2023-02-01', 'Scheduled');

title: Update Provider's Specialty
description: Dr. Jane Smith (provider_id 'prov_002') has recently completed advanced training and her specialty needs to be updated from 'Pediatrics' to 'Pediatric Cardiology'. This change ensures her profile is accurate. Update the specialty of provider 'prov_002' to 'Pediatric Cardiology'.
solution: UPDATE providers SET specialty = 'Pediatric Cardiology' WHERE provider_id = 'prov_002';

title: Delete Erroneous Patient Record
description: A duplicate patient record was mistakenly inserted into the `patients` table with a `patient_id` of \'p_err_01\'. Write a query to delete this specific row.
solution: DELETE FROM patients WHERE patient_id = 'p_err_01';

title: Purge Old Appointments
description: To free up database space, delete all rows from the `appointments` table where the `status` is entirely set to 'Cancelled' and the `appointment_date` is before '2020-01-01'.
solution: DELETE FROM appointments WHERE status = 'Cancelled' AND appointment_date < '2020-01-01';

title: Clinic Networks
description: Model a network of clinics and their doctors. Provide a `clinics` table with `clinic_id` as an in-line INTEGER PRIMARY KEY and `clinic_name` (TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE). Second, create a `doctors` table with `doctor_id` (INTEGER), `name` (TEXT NOT NULL), `specialty` (TEXT), and `clinic_id` (INTEGER). Use out-of-line constraints for `doctors` to set `doctor_id` as the PRIMARY KEY and `clinic_id` as a FOREIGN KEY referencing `clinics(clinic_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE clinics (clinic_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, clinic_name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE); CREATE TABLE doctors (doctor_id INTEGER, name TEXT NOT NULL, specialty TEXT, clinic_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (doctor_id), FOREIGN KEY (clinic_id) REFERENCES clinics(clinic_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('clinics') UNION ALL SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('doctors') UNION ALL SELECT "from", "table", 0, 0 FROM pragma_foreign_key_list('doctors');

title: Pharmacy Medications
description: Organize a pharmacy database. Construct a `pharmacies` table with an in-line `pharmacy_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY), `location` (TEXT NOT NULL), and `license_no` (TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL). Second, create a `medications` table with `med_id` (INTEGER), `drug_name` (TEXT NOT NULL), `stock` (INTEGER DEFAULT 0), and `pharmacy_id` (INTEGER). Apply out-of-line constraints to `medications` specifying `med_id` as the PRIMARY KEY, a CHECK that `stock >= 0`, and a FOREIGN KEY linking `pharmacy_id` to `pharmacies(pharmacy_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE pharmacies (pharmacy_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, location TEXT NOT NULL, license_no TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE medications (med_id INTEGER, drug_name TEXT NOT NULL, stock INTEGER DEFAULT 0, pharmacy_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (med_id), CHECK (stock >= 0), FOREIGN KEY (pharmacy_id) REFERENCES pharmacies(pharmacy_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('pharmacies') UNION ALL SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('medications') UNION ALL SELECT "from", "table", 0, 0 FROM pragma_foreign_key_list('medications');

title: Patient Vital Signs
description: Formulate a schema for patient care. Build a `wards` table with an in-line `ward_id` (VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY) and `max_capacity` (INTEGER NOT NULL). Second, construct a `vital_signs` table carrying `reading_id` (BIGINT), `temperature_c` (DECIMAL(4,1)), `heart_rate` (INTEGER), and `ward_id` (VARCHAR(10)). For `vital_signs`, define out-of-line requirements: PRIMARY KEY for `reading_id`, a CHECK constraint ensuring `heart_rate BETWEEN 0 AND 300`, and a FOREIGN KEY targeting `ward_id` to `wards(ward_id)`. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE wards (ward_id VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, max_capacity INTEGER NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE vital_signs (reading_id BIGINT, temperature_c DECIMAL(4,1), heart_rate INTEGER, ward_id VARCHAR(10), PRIMARY KEY (reading_id), CHECK (heart_rate BETWEEN 0 AND 300), FOREIGN KEY (ward_id) REFERENCES wards(ward_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('wards') UNION ALL SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('vital_signs') UNION ALL SELECT "from", "table", 0, 0 FROM pragma_foreign_key_list('vital_signs');

title: Patients with Multiple Providers
description: The care coordination team wants to identify patients who are seeing multiple specialists, which might indicate complex health needs or potential for care fragmentation. They need a list of such patients. Find the first name and last name of patients who have had appointments with more than one unique provider.
solution: SELECT p.first_name, p.last_name FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.patient_id = a.patient_id GROUP BY p.patient_id, p.first_name, p.last_name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT a.provider_id) > 1;

title: Providers Without Any Appointments
description: The hospital management needs to identify providers who have not yet been assigned any appointments. This could indicate new staff, specialists with low demand, or data entry issues. List the first name and last name of providers who have not had any appointments.
solution: SELECT pr.first_name, pr.last_name FROM providers pr LEFT JOIN appointments a ON pr.provider_id = a.provider_id WHERE a.appointment_id IS NULL;

title: Top 3 Most Prescribed Medications
description: The pharmacy department wants to analyze medication usage trends to optimize inventory and identify high-demand drugs. They need to know which medications are prescribed most often. Find the top 3 medication names that have been prescribed most frequently. Name the result column 'prescription_count'.
solution: SELECT medication_name, COUNT(prescription_id) AS prescription_count FROM prescriptions GROUP BY medication_name ORDER BY prescription_count DESC LIMIT 3;

title: Patients with Specific Diagnosis
description: For a research study, a medical researcher needs to identify all patients who have ever received a specific diagnosis, identified by its ICD code. This helps in patient cohort selection. Retrieve the first name and last name of patients who have received a diagnosis with ICD code 'J45'.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT p.first_name, p.last_name FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.patient_id = a.patient_id JOIN diagnoses d ON a.appointment_id = d.appointment_id WHERE d.icd_code = 'J45';

title: Appointments with Multiple Diagnoses
description: The medical coding and billing department needs to identify appointments where multiple diagnoses were recorded. This often indicates more complex cases and affects billing procedures. List all appointment IDs where a patient has multiple diagnoses.
solution: SELECT appointment_id FROM diagnoses GROUP BY appointment_id HAVING COUNT(diagnosis_id) > 1;

title: Patients Seeing Cardiology Specialists
description: The cardiology department wants to track all patients who have been seen by their specialists. This helps in understanding their patient base and referral patterns. Find the first name and last name of patients who have had appointments with a 'Cardiology' specialist.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT p.first_name, p.last_name FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.patient_id = a.patient_id JOIN providers pr ON a.provider_id = pr.provider_id WHERE pr.specialty = 'Cardiology';

title: Providers Prescribing Many Unique Medications
description: The pharmacy oversight committee wants to identify providers who prescribe a wide variety of medications, which might warrant a review of their prescribing habits. Identify providers who have prescribed more than 2 unique medications. Name the result column 'unique_med_count'.
solution: SELECT pr.first_name, pr.last_name, COUNT(DISTINCT pres.medication_name) AS unique_med_count FROM providers pr JOIN appointments a ON pr.provider_id = a.provider_id JOIN prescriptions pres ON a.appointment_id = pres.appointment_id GROUP BY pr.provider_id, pr.first_name, pr.last_name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT pres.medication_name) > 2;

title: Future Appointments
description: The scheduling department needs a clear view of all upcoming appointments to prepare resources and send reminders. This requires filtering out past appointments. List all appointments that are scheduled for a date after '2023-01-20'.
solution: SELECT * FROM appointments WHERE appointment_date > '2023-01-20' ORDER BY appointment_date;

title: Diagnosed but Unprescribed Appointments
description: For quality assurance, the medical review board wants to find appointments where a diagnosis was made but no prescription was issued. This could indicate a non-medication treatment plan or a potential oversight. List the appointment IDs where a diagnosis exists but no prescription was given for that same appointment.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT d.appointment_id FROM diagnoses d LEFT JOIN prescriptions p ON d.appointment_id = p.appointment_id WHERE p.prescription_id IS NULL;

title: Provider with Most Unique Patients
description: The hospital's marketing team wants to identify providers who have a broad patient base, indicating high patient trust or referral rates. They need to find the provider who has seen the most unique patients. Find the first name and last name of the provider who has seen the most unique patients. Name the result column 'unique_patient_count'.
solution: SELECT pr.first_name, pr.last_name, COUNT(DISTINCT a.patient_id) AS unique_patient_count FROM providers pr JOIN appointments a ON pr.provider_id = a.provider_id GROUP BY pr.provider_id, pr.first_name, pr.last_name ORDER BY unique_patient_count DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Patients Seen by Multiple Specialties
description: The patient care coordinator needs to identify patients who are receiving care from specialists in both Cardiology and Pediatrics. This helps in coordinating complex care plans. List the first name and last name of patients who have had appointments with both a 'Cardiology' and a 'Pediatrics' specialist.
solution: SELECT p.first_name, p.last_name FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.patient_id = a.patient_id JOIN providers pr ON a.provider_id = pr.provider_id WHERE pr.specialty = 'Cardiology' INTERSECT SELECT p2.first_name, p2.last_name FROM patients p2 JOIN appointments a2 ON p2.patient_id = a2.patient_id JOIN providers pr2 ON a2.provider_id = pr2.provider_id WHERE pr2.specialty = 'Pediatrics';

title: List All Users
description: As a platform administrator, you need to get a complete overview of all registered users on the streaming service. This will help in understanding the user base size. Retrieve all columns for every user in the database.
solution: SELECT * FROM users;

title: Find Premium Subscribers
description: The marketing team wants to identify all users who are currently on the 'Premium' subscription tier to offer them exclusive content. This helps in targeted promotions. Retrieve the username and email of all users with a 'Premium' subscription.
solution: SELECT username, email FROM users WHERE subscription_tier = 'Premium';

title: Tracks Released After 2020
description: A music critic is interested in reviewing only the newest music available on the platform. They want to focus on tracks released in recent years. Retrieve the title and release year of all tracks released in or after the year 2020.
solution: SELECT title, release_year FROM tracks WHERE release_year >= 2020;

title: Artists in the 'Pop' Genre
description: The content curation team is planning a 'Pop Hits' playlist and needs a list of all artists categorized under the 'Pop' genre. This ensures they include relevant artists. Retrieve the name and genre of all artists whose genre is 'Pop'.
solution: SELECT name, genre FROM artists WHERE genre = 'Pop';

title: Public Playlists
description: To promote user-generated content, the platform wants to showcase all public playlists. This allows other users to discover and enjoy shared music collections. Retrieve the playlist_id, title, and user_id for all playlists that are marked as public.
solution: SELECT playlist_id, title, user_id FROM playlists WHERE is_public = TRUE;

title: Long Tracks (Over 300 Seconds)
description: Some users prefer longer, more immersive tracks for their listening sessions. The platform wants to identify these tracks to create a 'Long Play' category. Retrieve the title, artist_id, and duration_seconds for all tracks that are longer than 300 seconds (5 minutes).
solution: SELECT title, artist_id, duration_seconds FROM tracks WHERE duration_seconds > 300;

title: Tracks with 'Remix' in Title
description: A DJ is looking for all 'remix' versions of songs to include in their next set. They need to quickly filter through the vast music library. Retrieve the track_id, title, and artist_id for all tracks whose title contains the word 'Remix' (case-insensitive).
solution: SELECT track_id, title, artist_id FROM tracks WHERE title LIKE '%Remix%' OR title LIKE '%remix%';

title: Users Joined in 2023
description: The analytics team wants to analyze user growth for specific periods. They are particularly interested in the cohort of users who joined last year. Retrieve the username, email, and join_date for all users who joined in the year 2023.
solution: SELECT username, email, join_date FROM users WHERE join_date LIKE '2023%';

title: Boolean Types in SQLite
description: In SQLite, the BOOLEAN data type is actually stored as an integer, where 1 represents true and 0 represents false. We need to find all tracks that a user actually finished listening to. Find the user_id and track_id of records in `listening_history` where `completed` is true.
solution: SELECT user_id, track_id FROM listening_history WHERE completed = 1;

title: Total Number of Tracks
description: The content management team needs to know the total size of the music library. This helps in reporting and capacity planning. Retrieve the total count of all tracks available on the platform. Name the result column 'total_tracks'.
solution: SELECT COUNT(track_id) AS total_tracks FROM tracks;

title: Tracks Per Artist
description: To understand artist contributions, the A&R department wants to see how many tracks each artist has released. This helps in identifying prolific artists. Retrieve the artist's name and the count of their tracks, ordered by the number of tracks in descending order. (Only include artists who have released at least one track). Name the result column 'number_of_tracks'.
solution: SELECT a.name, COUNT(t.track_id) AS number_of_tracks FROM artists a JOIN tracks t ON a.artist_id = t.artist_id GROUP BY a.name ORDER BY number_of_tracks DESC;

title: Average Track Duration
description: The sound engineering team is analyzing the typical length of songs on the platform. This data can inform future content acquisition strategies. Retrieve the average duration (in seconds) of all tracks. Name the result column 'average_track_duration'.
solution: SELECT AVG(duration_seconds) AS average_track_duration FROM tracks;

title: Total Listening Time Per User
description: The user engagement team wants to identify 'power users' by calculating their total listening time. This metric helps in understanding user loyalty and activity. Retrieve the username and their total listening time in minutes (sum of `duration_seconds` from completed listens, divided by 60). Name the result column 'total_listening_minutes'.
solution: SELECT u.username, SUM(t.duration_seconds) / 60 AS total_listening_minutes FROM users u JOIN listening_history lh ON u.user_id = lh.user_id JOIN tracks t ON lh.track_id = t.track_id WHERE lh.completed = TRUE GROUP BY u.username ORDER BY total_listening_minutes DESC;

title: Users Who Listened to More Than 5 Songs
description: The analytics team defines 'active users' as those who have listened to a significant number of songs. They want to identify users who have listened to more than 5 tracks. Retrieve the username and the count of tracks they have listened to, for users who have listened to more than 5 tracks. Name the result column 'tracks_listened'.
solution: SELECT u.username, COUNT(lh.track_id) AS tracks_listened FROM users u JOIN listening_history lh ON u.user_id = lh.user_id GROUP BY u.username HAVING COUNT(lh.track_id) > 5 ORDER BY tracks_listened DESC;

title: Tracks in a Specific Playlist
description: A user wants to see all the tracks contained within one of their playlists, specifically 'My Top Hits'. This helps them manage and share their curated music. Retrieve the title of all tracks that are part of the playlist titled 'My Top Hits'.
solution: SELECT t.title FROM tracks t JOIN listening_history lh ON t.track_id = lh.track_id JOIN playlists p ON lh.user_id = p.user_id WHERE p.title = 'My Top Hits' AND p.playlist_id = (SELECT playlist_id FROM playlists WHERE title = 'My Top Hits' LIMIT 1);

title: Artists with More Than 3 Tracks
description: The platform wants to highlight artists with a substantial presence in the music library. They are looking for artists who have released more than 3 tracks. Retrieve the name of artists who have more than 3 tracks, along with their track count. Name the result column 'track_count'.
solution: SELECT a.name, COUNT(t.track_id) AS track_count FROM artists a JOIN tracks t ON a.artist_id = t.artist_id GROUP BY a.name HAVING COUNT(t.track_id) > 3 ORDER BY track_count DESC;

title: Most Popular Genres by Track Count
description: The content acquisition team wants to understand which music genres are most prevalent on the platform. This helps in identifying trends and gaps in the library. Retrieve the genre and the total number of tracks for each genre, ordered by track count descending. (Only include genres with at least one track). Name the result column 'track_count'.

Assign out-of-line constraints to the `books` table: `book_id` as PRIMARY KEY, a CHECK enforcing `pages > 0`, and a FOREIGN KEY allowing `pub_id` to reference `publishers(pub_id)`.

title: Top 3 Most Listened Tracks
description: The platform wants to showcase its most popular content. They need to identify the top 3 tracks based on the number of times they have been listened to. Retrieve the title of the top 3 most listened tracks along with their listen count. Name the result column 'listen_count'.
solution: SELECT t.title, COUNT(lh.track_id) AS listen_count FROM tracks t JOIN listening_history lh ON t.track_id = lh.track_id GROUP BY t.title ORDER BY listen_count DESC LIMIT 3;

title: Users with Multiple Public Playlists
description: The community management team wants to recognize users who actively contribute to the platform by creating public playlists. They are looking for users who have created more than one public playlist. Retrieve the username of users who have created more than one public playlist.
solution: SELECT u.username FROM users u JOIN playlists p ON u.user_id = p.user_id WHERE p.is_public = TRUE GROUP BY u.username HAVING COUNT(p.playlist_id) > 1;

title: Artists Whose Average Track Duration Exceeds Global Average
description: The A&R team is looking for artists who tend to produce longer-form music, potentially indicating a specific artistic style. They want to find artists whose average track duration is greater than the overall average track duration across the platform. Retrieve the artist's name and their average track duration. Name the result column 'artist_avg_duration'.
solution: SELECT a.name, AVG(t.duration_seconds) AS artist_avg_duration FROM artists a JOIN tracks t ON a.artist_id = t.artist_id GROUP BY a.name HAVING AVG(t.duration_seconds) > (SELECT AVG(duration_seconds) FROM tracks);

title: Rank Users by Total Listening Time
description: The analytics team wants to identify the most engaged users by ranking them based on their total listening time. This helps in understanding user engagement levels. Retrieve the username and their total listening time in minutes, along with their rank. Name the result column 'total_listening_minutes'.
solution:
WITH UserListening AS (
  SELECT
    u.username,
    SUM(t.duration_seconds) / 60 AS total_listening_minutes
  FROM users u
  JOIN listening_history lh ON u.user_id = lh.user_id
  JOIN tracks t ON lh.track_id = t.track_id
  WHERE lh.completed = TRUE
  GROUP BY u.username
)
SELECT
  username,
  total_listening_minutes,
  RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_listening_minutes DESC) AS listening_rank
FROM UserListening;

solution: SELECT t.title FROM tracks t JOIN listening_history lh ON t.track_id = lh.track_id GROUP BY t.title HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT lh.user_id || lh.track_id) > 1;
