In addition, create a `units_new` table consisting of `unit_id` (INTEGER), `unit_number` (TEXT NOT NULL), `sq_ft` (REAL), and `bldg_id` (INTEGER). Within the `units_new` table, lay out out-of-line requirements: `unit_id` as PRIMARY KEY, a CHECK ensuring `sq_ft >= 100`, and a FOREIGN KEY pointing `bldg_id` to `buildings(bldg_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE buildings (bldg_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, address TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, floors INTEGER); CREATE TABLE units_new (unit_id INTEGER, unit_number TEXT NOT NULL, sq_ft REAL, bldg_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (unit_id), CHECK (sq_ft >= 100), FOREIGN KEY (bldg_id) REFERENCES buildings(bldg_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Commercial Leases
description: Establish a commercial real estate lease system. Provide a `commercial_zones` table with an in-line `zone_code` (VARCHAR(20) PRIMARY KEY) and `zone_multiplier` (DECIMAL(3,2) NOT NULL). Then map out a `leases` table holding `lease_id` (BIGINT), `tenant_name` (VARCHAR(150) NOT NULL), `monthly_rent` (DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL), and `zone_code` (VARCHAR(20)). Finally, in `leases`, configure out-of-line constraints for a PRIMARY KEY on `lease_id`, a CHECK verifying `monthly_rent >= 500.00`, and a FOREIGN KEY joining `zone_code` to `commercial_zones(zone_code)`. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE commercial_zones (zone_code VARCHAR(20) PRIMARY KEY, zone_multiplier DECIMAL(3,2) NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE leases (lease_id BIGINT, tenant_name VARCHAR(150) NOT NULL, monthly_rent DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL, zone_code VARCHAR(20), PRIMARY KEY (lease_id), CHECK (monthly_rent >= 500.00), FOREIGN KEY (zone_code) REFERENCES commercial_zones(zone_code));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Properties within a Radial Distance
description: A new commercial hub is planned at a specific location, and investors are interested in properties within a short commuting distance. This requires a radial search to identify nearby properties. Retrieve properties located within a radial distance of 0.05 units from the point (latitude 34.05, longitude -118.25).
solution: SELECT * FROM properties WHERE (latitude - 34.05)*(latitude - 34.05) + (longitude - -118.25)*(longitude - -118.25) < 0.05*0.05;

title: High-Value Neighborhoods
description: An analytics team wants to identify neighborhoods that consistently feature high-value properties, indicating a premium market. This involves calculating average prices within each neighborhood's bounding box and filtering. Retrieve the names of neighborhoods where the average price of properties within their bounding box is greater than 1000000.
solution: SELECT n.neighborhood_name FROM neighborhoods n JOIN properties p ON p.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude GROUP BY n.neighborhood_name HAVING AVG(p.price) > 1000000 ORDER BY n.neighborhood_name;

title: Properties Not in Any Defined Neighborhood
description: Sometimes properties are listed that fall outside the boundaries of defined neighborhoods, which might indicate new development areas or data inconsistencies. It's important to identify these 'unassigned' properties. Retrieve properties that do not fall within the bounding box of any defined neighborhood.
solution: SELECT p.* FROM properties p WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM neighborhoods n WHERE p.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude);

title: Most Expensive Property Per Neighborhood
description: To highlight premium listings, a marketing team wants to identify the single most expensive property within each neighborhood. This requires finding the maximum price per group and then retrieving the corresponding property. Retrieve the neighborhood name, address, and price of the most expensive property within each neighborhood's bounding box.
solution: SELECT n.neighborhood_name, p.address, p.price FROM neighborhoods n JOIN properties p ON p.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude WHERE p.price = (SELECT MAX(p2.price) FROM properties p2 WHERE p2.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p2.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude) ORDER BY n.neighborhood_name;

title: Large Properties in Specific Neighborhoods
description: A family is looking for larger homes in well-established neighborhoods. They need properties with more than 3 bedrooms that are definitely located within a defined neighborhood. Retrieve properties that are within a neighborhood's bounding box AND have more than 3 bedrooms.
solution: SELECT p.*, n.neighborhood_name FROM properties p JOIN neighborhoods n ON p.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude WHERE p.bedrooms > 3 ORDER BY n.neighborhood_name, p.bedrooms DESC;

title: Properties Above Average for Their Bedroom Count
description: An analyst wants to identify properties that are priced higher than the average for properties with the same number of bedrooms. This helps in spotting potentially overvalued listings or premium offerings. Retrieve properties whose price is above the average price of properties with the same number of bedrooms. Name the result column 'avg_price'.
solution: SELECT p.property_id, p.address, p.price, p.bedrooms FROM properties p JOIN (SELECT bedrooms, AVG(price) AS avg_price FROM properties GROUP BY bedrooms) AS sub ON p.bedrooms = sub.bedrooms WHERE p.price > sub.avg_price ORDER BY p.bedrooms, p.price DESC;

title: Neighborhoods with Only Large Properties
description: A luxury real estate agency is interested in neighborhoods exclusively featuring larger properties, indicating a market segment for spacious homes. They need to find neighborhoods where all properties (within their bounding box) have at least 2 bedrooms. Retrieve the names of neighborhoods where every property located within its bounding box has 2 or more bedrooms.
solution: SELECT n.neighborhood_name FROM neighborhoods n WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM properties p WHERE p.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude AND p.bedrooms < 2) AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM properties p WHERE p.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude) ORDER BY n.neighborhood_name;

title: Underpriced Properties in High-Value Neighborhoods
description: An investor is looking for potential deals: properties in desirable neighborhoods that are currently listed below the average price for that specific neighborhood. This requires comparing individual property prices to neighborhood averages. Retrieve properties that are within a neighborhood's bounding box but have a price lower than the overall average property price in that specific neighborhood. Name the result column 'avg_neighborhood_price'.
solution: SELECT p.address, p.price, n.neighborhood_name, sub.avg_neighborhood_price FROM properties p JOIN neighborhoods n ON p.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude JOIN (SELECT n2.neighborhood_id, AVG(p2.price) AS avg_neighborhood_price FROM neighborhoods n2 JOIN properties p2 ON p2.latitude BETWEEN n2.min_latitude AND n2.max_latitude AND p2.longitude BETWEEN n2.min_longitude AND n2.max_longitude GROUP BY n2.neighborhood_id) AS sub ON n.neighborhood_id = sub.neighborhood_id WHERE p.price < sub.avg_neighborhood_price ORDER BY n.neighborhood_name, p.price;

title: Properties in 'Downtown' and Near a Landmark
description: A client is looking for properties specifically in the 'Downtown' neighborhood that are also very close to a central landmark, combining both neighborhood preference and proximity. This requires a combined bounding box and radial search. Retrieve properties that are within the bounding box of the 'Downtown' neighborhood AND also within a 0.05 radial distance of the point (latitude 34.05, longitude -118.25).
solution: SELECT p.* FROM properties p JOIN neighborhoods n ON n.neighborhood_name = 'Downtown' WHERE p.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude AND (p.latitude - 34.05)*(p.latitude - 34.05) + (p.longitude - -118.25)*(p.longitude - -118.25) < 0.05*0.05;

title: All Species
description: A new conservation biologist has just joined our wildlife preserve team and needs a comprehensive understanding of all the flora and fauna we monitor. To get them up to speed, we need a complete inventory. Write a query to retrieve all information about every species tracked in the preserve.
solution: SELECT * FROM species;

title: Endangered Species
description: Our conservation efforts are focused on protecting the most vulnerable species within the preserve. To allocate resources effectively for immediate intervention and monitoring, we need a clear list of those at highest risk. Find the common names of all species whose conservation status is 'Endangered'.
solution: SELECT common_name FROM species WHERE conservation_status = 'Endangered';

title: Large Mammals Researchers
description: A new grant has just been approved for a critical large mammal conservation project, and the project lead needs to quickly assemble a team. Write a query to find the first and last names of all researchers whose specialty is 'Large Mammals'.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name FROM researchers WHERE specialty = 'Large Mammals';

title: Alpine Habitats
description: Our research team is planning an expedition to study high-altitude flora and fauna. We need a list of all designated 'Alpine' zones to prepare our itinerary. Write a query to retrieve the zone_id and zone_name of all zones that are of 'Alpine' habitat_type.
solution: SELECT zone_id, zone_name FROM zones WHERE habitat_type = 'Alpine';

title: Vulnerable Species
description: Conservationists are prioritizing efforts to protect endangered wildlife. We need to identify all species currently classified as 'Vulnerable' to focus our resources. Find the common_name and scientific_name of all species with a 'Vulnerable' conservation_status.
solution: SELECT common_name, scientific_name FROM species WHERE conservation_status = 'Vulnerable';

title: Warm Sightings
description: Biologists are investigating the impact of warmer temperatures on species behavior. We need to analyze sightings that occurred during relatively warm conditions. Retrieve all fields from sightings where the temperature is greater than 10 degrees Celsius, ordered by temperature descending.
solution: SELECT * FROM sightings WHERE temperature_celsius > 10 ORDER BY temperature_celsius DESC;

title: Top 3 Coldest Sightings
description: Climatologists are studying extreme weather events and their effect on wildlife. We need to pinpoint the coldest recorded sightings to understand species resilience. Find the sighting_id and temperature_celsius of the 3 sightings with the lowest recorded temperatures.
solution: SELECT sighting_id, temperature_celsius FROM sightings ORDER BY temperature_celsius ASC LIMIT 3;

title: All Sightings Order
description: When analyzing large datasets of ecological observations, a consistent order is crucial for review and further processing. This helps in systematically reviewing a researcher's complete field log. Write a query to retrieve all sightings ordered by researcher_id ascending, and then by sighting_id ascending.
solution: SELECT * FROM sightings ORDER BY researcher_id ASC, sighting_id ASC;

title: Select Specific Sightings
description: A specific researcher, 'r4', has reported some unusual observations, and we need to quickly review their recent findings. This helps us focus on their specific contributions to a particular study. Find the sighting_id and health_status of all sightings made by researcher 'r4'.
solution: SELECT sighting_id, health_status FROM sightings WHERE researcher_id = 'r4';

title: Sightings by Zone
description: Our ecological researchers are analyzing spatial patterns of wildlife activity and need to understand exactly where each observation took place within the preserve's designated zones. To map out species distribution accurately, we need to link sightings to their geographical context. Write a query to join sightings with zones and retrieve the sighting_id and the zone_name where it occurred.
solution: SELECT s.sighting_id, z.zone_name FROM sightings s JOIN zones z ON s.zone_id = z.zone_id;

title: Total Sightings per Species
description: The wildlife management team is preparing its annual report and needs to quantify the observation frequency for each species to assess population health and monitoring effectiveness. We need a summary of how often each species has been sighted across the preserve. Count the number of sightings for each species. Select the common_name and the count as total_sightings.
solution: SELECT sp.common_name, COUNT(s.sighting_id) as total_sightings FROM species sp LEFT JOIN sightings s ON sp.species_id = s.species_id GROUP BY sp.common_name;

title: Average Sighting Temperature
description: To better understand the environmental context of animal observations, researchers want to know the typical temperature conditions. Calculate the average temperature of all sightings. Return the result as avg_temp rounded to 1 decimal place.
solution: SELECT ROUND(AVG(temperature_celsius), 1) as avg_temp FROM sightings;

title: Researchers and their Sightings
description: Join researchers and sightings to retrieve the researcher's last name and the health status of the animal sighted. Include researchers even if they have no sightings.
solution: SELECT r.last_name, s.health_status FROM researchers r LEFT JOIN sightings s ON r.researcher_id = s.researcher_id;

title: Total Grassland Sightings
description: Ecologists are assessing biodiversity across different ecosystems. We need to quantify the observational effort in 'Grassland' habitats to compare with other zones. Count the total number of sightings that occurred in a Grassland habitat. Return the habitat_type and the result as sighting_count.
solution: SELECT z.habitat_type, COUNT(s.sighting_id) as sighting_count FROM sightings s JOIN zones z ON s.zone_id = z.zone_id WHERE z.habitat_type = 'Grassland' GROUP BY z.habitat_type;

title: Unique Active Researchers
description: The research coordinator wants to evaluate team engagement and identify all researchers who have actively contributed data to the sighting database. Count how many distinct researchers have made at least one sighting. Return as active_researchers.
solution: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT researcher_id) as active_researchers FROM sightings;

title: Sightings Per Species
description: To understand population trends and observation frequency, we need a summary of how many times each species has been sighted across all expeditions. For each species, list its common_name and the total number of sightings. Name the count 'total_sightings'. Group by common_name.
solution: SELECT sp.common_name, COUNT(s.sighting_id) as total_sightings FROM species sp LEFT JOIN sightings s ON sp.species_id = s.species_id GROUP BY sp.common_name;

title: Average Temp Per Zone
description: Calculate the average temperature recorded in each zone. Return zone_name and the average wrapped as avg_temp rounded to 1 decimal place. Order alphabetically by zone_name. (Only include zones that have at least one sighting).
solution: SELECT z.zone_name, ROUND(AVG(s.temperature_celsius), 1) as avg_temp FROM zones z JOIN sightings s ON z.zone_id = s.zone_id GROUP BY z.zone_name ORDER BY z.zone_name;

title: No Sightings Yet
description: The project manager needs to follow up with new team members. We need to identify researchers who haven't yet submitted any sighting data. Find the first and last name of any researchers who have NOT logged any sightings.
solution: SELECT r.first_name, r.last_name FROM researchers r LEFT JOIN sightings s ON r.researcher_id = s.researcher_id WHERE s.sighting_id IS NULL;

title: Min and Max Temperatures
description: Understanding the environmental extremes observed during fieldwork is vital for assessing species resilience and habitat suitability. This helps us gauge the overall temperature range experienced by the observed wildlife. Find the minimum and maximum temperatures across all sightings. Return them as min_temp and max_temp.
solution: SELECT MIN(temperature_celsius) as min_temp, MAX(temperature_celsius) as max_temp FROM sightings;

title: Healthy vs Sick Animals
description: Monitoring the health of wildlife populations is a key aspect of ecological research and conservation. We need to quickly assess the prevalence of healthy versus sick animals reported in our sightings data. Group the sightings by health_status and count how many sightings fall into each category. Return health_status and the count as status_count.
solution: SELECT health_status, COUNT(*) as status_count FROM sightings GROUP BY health_status;

title: Sightings By Specialty
description: Find out how many sightings were recorded by researchers of each specialty. Join researchers and sightings, grouped by specialty. Return specialty and sighting_count. (Only include specialties that have recorded at least one sighting).
solution: SELECT r.specialty, COUNT(s.sighting_id) as sighting_count FROM researchers r JOIN sightings s ON r.researcher_id = s.researcher_id GROUP BY r.specialty;

title: New Discovery
description: Insert a new species into the species table. Use 's4' for species_id, 'Bald Eagle' for common_name, 'Haliaeetus leucocephalus' for scientific_name, and 'Least Concern' for conservation_status.
solution: INSERT INTO species (species_id, common_name, scientific_name, conservation_status) VALUES ('s4', 'Bald Eagle', 'Haliaeetus leucocephalus', 'Least Concern');

title: Update Health Status
description: Update the health status of sighting 'st1' to 'Healthy'.
solution: UPDATE sightings SET health_status = 'Healthy' WHERE sighting_id = 'st1';

title: Add New Zone
description: Insert a new zone into the zones table. Use 'z6' for zone_id, 'Blacktail Plateau' for zone_name, and 'Grassland' for habitat_type.
solution: INSERT INTO zones (zone_id, zone_name, habitat_type) VALUES ('z6', 'Blacktail Plateau', 'Grassland');

title: Update Conservation Status
description: Update the conservation status of species 's3' to 'Least Concern'.
solution: UPDATE species SET conservation_status = 'Least Concern' WHERE species_id = 's3';

title: Delete Sighting
description: During data quality review, it was discovered that sighting 'st12' was a duplicate entry. We need to remove this erroneous record to maintain data integrity. Sighting 'st12' was recorded in error. DELETE it from sightings, then return the count of remaining sightings as current_count.
solution: DELETE FROM sightings WHERE sighting_id = 'st12';

title: Reassign Sightings
description: Researcher 'r1' is taking over all sightings from 'r2'. Update the researcher_id of all sightings by 'r2' to 'r1'. Then SELECT all sightings from 'r1'.
solution: UPDATE sightings SET researcher_id = 'r1' WHERE researcher_id = 'r2';

title: Remove Geyser Basin Sightings
description: A recent data audit revealed that sightings from the 'Geyser Basin' zone were collected using faulty equipment, making them unreliable. We need to remove this erroneous data to maintain data integrity. Delete all sightings that occurred in 'Geyser Basin' (which has zone_id 'z1'). Then return all remaining sightings.
solution: DELETE FROM sightings WHERE zone_id = 'z1';

title: New Equipment Tracking
description: The research team has just acquired a suite of new, specialized equipment for their upcoming field studies and needs a robust way to track these valuable assets. Create a new table named 'equipment' with columns: 'equipment_id' (TEXT PRIMARY KEY) and 'name' (TEXT).
solution: CREATE TABLE equipment (equipment_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT);

title: Add Certification Column
description: To ensure proper project assignments and maintain compliance with safety protocols, the research institute needs to track the various certification levels of its researchers. Add a new column called 'certification_level' of type TEXT to the researchers table.
solution: ALTER TABLE researchers ADD COLUMN certification_level TEXT;

title: Create Vehicles Table
description: To better manage logistics for field expeditions, the research facility needs to track its fleet of vehicles and assign them to researchers. Create a 'vehicles' table with 'vehicle_id' (TEXT PRIMARY KEY), 'license_plate' (TEXT), and 'vehicle_type' (TEXT).
solution: CREATE TABLE vehicles (vehicle_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, license_plate TEXT, vehicle_type TEXT);

title: Research Email Contact
description: To improve communication and collaboration among the research team, we need a standardized way to store and access their contact information. Add a new column 'email' of type TEXT to the researchers table so they can be contacted easily.
solution: ALTER TABLE researchers ADD COLUMN email TEXT;

title: Drop Equipment Table
description: Our organization is centralizing equipment management into a new, dedicated inventory system. To avoid redundancy and potential data conflicts, the old 'equipment' table is no longer needed in this database. We are moving equipment tracking to another database. Drop the 'equipment' table if it exists.
solution: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS equipment;
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Create Funding Sources
description: To better track the financial support for our research projects, we need a dedicated table to manage grant information. This will help us monitor funding sources and allocated amounts. Create a new table named 'funding' with columns: 'grant_id' (TEXT PRIMARY KEY), 'source_name' (TEXT), and 'amount' (INTEGER).
solution: CREATE TABLE funding (grant_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, source_name TEXT, amount INTEGER);

title: Research Facilities
description: Design the schema for research facilities. Create a `laboratories` table with `lab_id` as an in-line INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, `name` (TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE), and `capacity` (INTEGER). Then create an `experiments` table with `exp_id` (INTEGER), `title` (TEXT NOT NULL), `budget` (REAL), and `lab_id` (INTEGER). Use an out-of-line PRIMARY KEY for `exp_id` and an out-of-line FOREIGN KEY for `lab_id` referencing `laboratories(lab_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE laboratories (lab_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, capacity INTEGER); CREATE TABLE experiments (exp_id INTEGER, title TEXT NOT NULL, budget REAL, lab_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (exp_id), FOREIGN KEY (lab_id) REFERENCES laboratories(lab_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Grant Funding Allocation
description: Design a schema for grant funding. Create a `grants` table with an in-line `grant_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY), `fund_name` (TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE), and `total_amount` (REAL NOT NULL). Then, create a `projects_new` table with `project_id` (INTEGER), `title` (TEXT NOT NULL), `status` (TEXT DEFAULT 'Active'), and `grant_id` (INTEGER). For `projects_new`, define out-of-line constraints: a PRIMARY KEY on `project_id`, a CHECK constraint ensuring `status IN ('Active', 'Completed')`, and a FOREIGN KEY on `grant_id` referencing `grants(grant_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE grants (grant_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, fund_name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, total_amount REAL NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE projects_new (project_id INTEGER, title TEXT NOT NULL, status TEXT DEFAULT 'Active', grant_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (project_id), CHECK (status IN ('Active', 'Completed')), FOREIGN KEY (grant_id) REFERENCES grants(grant_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('grants') UNION ALL SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('projects_new') UNION ALL SELECT "from", "table", 0, 0 FROM pragma_foreign_key_list('projects_new');

title: Chemical Compound Tracking
description: Design a schema for storing chemical components. Create an `elements` table with an in-line `atomic_number` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY) and `symbol` (VARCHAR(3) UNIQUE NOT NULL). Next, create a `compounds` table with `compound_id` (BIGINT), `formula` (VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL), `molar_mass` (DECIMAL(8,3) NOT NULL), and `base_element_id` (INTEGER). Apply out-of-line constraints in `compounds` for: PRIMARY KEY on `compound_id`, a CHECK ensuring `molar_mass > 0.0`, and a FOREIGN KEY mapping `base_element_id` to `elements(atomic_number)`. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE elements (atomic_number INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, symbol VARCHAR(3) UNIQUE NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE compounds (compound_id BIGINT, formula VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, molar_mass DECIMAL(8,3) NOT NULL, base_element_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (compound_id), CHECK (molar_mass > 0.0), FOREIGN KEY (base_element_id) REFERENCES elements(atomic_number));
verifyQuery: SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('elements') UNION ALL SELECT name, type, "notnull", pk FROM pragma_table_info('compounds') UNION ALL SELECT "from", "table", 0, 0 FROM pragma_foreign_key_list('compounds');

title: Changing Varchar Length
description: You need to change the max length of the `formula` column in `compounds` from VARCHAR(255) to VARCHAR(500). Since SQLite does not natively support altering column types, rename `compounds` to `compounds_old`. Then create a new `compounds` table matching the old one (`compound_id` BIGINT PRIMARY KEY, `formula` VARCHAR(500), `molar_mass` DECIMAL(8,3)), insert the old data into it, and drop `compounds_old`.
solution: ALTER TABLE compounds RENAME TO compounds_old; CREATE TABLE compounds (compound_id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY, formula VARCHAR(500), molar_mass DECIMAL(8,3)); INSERT INTO compounds SELECT compound_id, formula, molar_mass FROM compounds_old; DROP TABLE compounds_old;

title: Vulnerable Bear Sightings
description: Find the common_name of the species, the first and last name of the researcher, and the temperature for all sightings where the health status is 'Vulnerable'.
solution: SELECT sp.common_name, r.first_name, r.last_name, s.temperature_celsius FROM sightings s JOIN species sp ON s.species_id = sp.species_id JOIN researchers r ON s.researcher_id = r.researcher_id WHERE s.health_status = 'Vulnerable';

title: Healthy Animals in Grasslands
description: Conservationists are evaluating the impact of recent grassland habitat restoration projects and want to identify which healthy animal populations are successfully thriving in these areas. Find the common_name of animals sighted in a 'Grassland' habitat with a 'Healthy' status.
solution: SELECT sp.common_name FROM sightings s JOIN species sp ON s.species_id = sp.species_id JOIN zones z ON s.zone_id = z.zone_id WHERE z.habitat_type = 'Grassland' AND s.health_status = 'Healthy';

title: Cold Weather Sightings
description: A new research initiative is investigating the physiological and behavioral responses of animals to cold weather. Retrieve the researcher_id and the common name of the species for any sightings where the temperature was below 10 degrees Celsius.
solution: SELECT s.researcher_id, sp.common_name FROM sightings s JOIN species sp ON s.species_id = sp.species_id WHERE s.temperature_celsius < 10;

title: Highest Temperature Reporter
description: We're investigating extreme environmental conditions and want to acknowledge the researcher who documented the record-breaking high-temperature sighting. Find the first and last name of the researcher who recorded the sighting with the absolute highest temperature in the database.
solution: SELECT r.first_name, r.last_name FROM researchers r JOIN sightings s ON r.researcher_id = s.researcher_id ORDER BY s.temperature_celsius DESC LIMIT 1;

title: At-Risk Sightings
description: Retrieve the sighting_id and the common_name of the species for any sightings where the animal was marked as 'Sick' or 'Injured', AND the species is 'Endangered'.
solution: SELECT s.sighting_id, sp.common_name FROM sightings s JOIN species sp ON s.species_id = sp.species_id WHERE s.health_status IN ('Sick', 'Injured') AND sp.conservation_status = 'Endangered';

title: Active Alpine Specialists
description: Conservation efforts often focus on specific ecosystems. To understand who is actively contributing to our knowledge of high-altitude biodiversity, we need to identify the researchers specializing in Alpine regions. Find the distinct last names of researchers who have recorded sightings in an 'Alpine' habitat.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT r.last_name FROM researchers r JOIN sightings s ON r.researcher_id = s.researcher_id JOIN zones z ON s.zone_id = z.zone_id WHERE z.habitat_type = 'Alpine';

title: Multi-Zone Species
description: Understanding species distribution across different ecological zones is crucial for conservation planning. We want to identify species that demonstrate adaptability or wide-ranging habits by appearing in multiple distinct habitats. Find the common_name of species that have been sighted in more than 1 distinct zone.
solution: SELECT sp.common_name FROM species sp JOIN sightings s ON sp.species_id = s.species_id GROUP BY sp.species_id, sp.common_name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT s.zone_id) > 1;

title: List All Teams
description: As a sports analyst, you need a quick overview of all participating teams in the league. This helps in understanding the current league structure. Retrieve the `team_id`, `name`, `city`, and `founded_year` for all teams.
solution: SELECT team_id, name, city, founded_year FROM teams;

title: Players by Position
description: The coaching staff wants to identify all players who play as a 'Forward' to analyze their performance and potential tactical adjustments. This helps in scouting and team strategy. Retrieve the `first_name`, `last_name`, and `jersey_number` of all players whose position is 'Forward'.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name, jersey_number FROM players WHERE position = 'Forward';

title: Venues in a City
description: A tournament organizer is planning an event and needs to know all available venues in 'London' to assess potential locations. This information is crucial for logistical planning. Retrieve the `name`, `city`, and `capacity` of all venues located in 'London'.
solution: SELECT name, city, capacity FROM venues WHERE city = 'London';

title: Matches on a Specific Date
description: The media team needs to compile highlights for matches played on '2023-10-26' for their broadcast. This requires filtering matches by their scheduled date. Retrieve the `match_id`, `home_score`, and `away_score` for all matches that took place on '2023-10-26'.
solution: SELECT match_id, home_score, away_score FROM matches WHERE match_date = '2023-10-26';

title: High Jersey Numbers
description: A fan club is looking for players with higher jersey numbers, specifically those greater than 20, for a special merchandise campaign. This helps in identifying a specific subset of players. Retrieve the `first_name`, `last_name`, and `jersey_number` for all players whose jersey number is greater than 20.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name, jersey_number FROM players WHERE jersey_number > 20;

title: Players of a Team
description: The scouting department wants a roster of all players belonging to team 'T001' to evaluate their squad depth. This provides a clear list of team members. Retrieve the `first_name`, `last_name`, `position`, and `jersey_number` for all players associated with `team_id` 'T001'.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name, position, jersey_number FROM players WHERE team_id = 'T001';

title: Home Team Wins
description: For statistical analysis, you need to identify all matches where the home team secured a victory. This helps in understanding home-field advantage trends. Retrieve the `match_id`, `home_score`, and `away_score` for all matches where the home team's score was greater than the away team's score.
solution: SELECT match_id, home_score, away_score FROM matches WHERE home_score > away_score;

title: Unique Venue Cities
description: To understand the geographical spread of the league, you need a list of all unique cities hosting venues. This helps in planning travel logistics and regional marketing. Retrieve a distinct list of `city` names from the `venues` table.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT city FROM venues;

title: Match Details with Team Names
description: The broadcast team needs a comprehensive list of matches, showing not just the team IDs but their actual names for on-screen graphics. This requires joining the `matches` table with the `teams` table twice. Retrieve the `match_id`, `match_date`, `home_score`, `away_score`, and the `name` of both the home team and the away team for all matches. Name the result column 'home_team_name'.
solution: SELECT m.match_id, m.match_date, th.name AS home_team_name, m.home_score, ta.name AS away_team_name, m.away_score FROM matches m JOIN teams th ON m.home_team_id = th.team_id JOIN teams ta ON m.away_team_id = ta.team_id;

title: Team Standings Points
description: To generate league standings, you need to calculate the total points for each team. A win earns 3 points, a tie earns 1 point, and a loss earns 0 points, considering both home and away matches. Retrieve the `team_id`, `name`, and their `total_points` for all teams.
solution: SELECT t.team_id, t.name, SUM(CASE WHEN m.home_team_id = t.team_id AND m.home_score > m.away_score THEN 3 WHEN m.away_team_id = t.team_id AND m.away_score > m.home_score THEN 3 WHEN m.home_team_id = t.team_id AND m.home_score = m.away_score THEN 1 WHEN m.away_team_id = t.team_id AND m.away_score = m.home_score THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS total_points FROM teams t JOIN matches m ON t.team_id = m.home_team_id OR t.team_id = m.away_team_id GROUP BY t.team_id, t.name ORDER BY total_points DESC;

title: Top Scorer in a Single Match
description: The league wants to recognize outstanding individual performances by identifying the player who scored the highest number of goals in any single match. This highlights exceptional offensive talent. Retrieve the `first_name`, `last_name`, and their `max_goals` in a match.
solution: SELECT p.first_name, p.last_name, MAX(sl.goals) AS max_goals FROM stats_log sl JOIN players p ON sl.player_id = p.player_id GROUP BY p.player_id ORDER BY max_goals DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Fairest Team (Lowest Yellow Cards)
description: The disciplinary committee wants to identify teams with the best fair play record, specifically those with the lowest average yellow cards per match. This promotes sportsmanship within the league. Retrieve the `team_id`, `name`, and their `average_yellow_cards` across all matches they participated in. Name the result column 'total_yellow_cards'.
solution: SELECT t.team_id, t.name, AVG(sub.total_yellow_cards) AS average_yellow_cards FROM teams t JOIN (SELECT m.match_id, p.team_id, SUM(sl.yellow_cards) AS total_yellow_cards FROM stats_log sl JOIN players p ON sl.player_id = p.player_id JOIN matches m ON sl.match_id = m.match_id GROUP BY m.match_id, p.team_id) AS sub ON t.team_id = sub.team_id GROUP BY t.team_id, t.name ORDER BY average_yellow_cards ASC LIMIT 1;

title: Total Goals Per Team
description: The analytics department needs to know the offensive power of each team. Calculate the total number of goals scored by each team, considering both their home and away matches. Retrieve the `team_id`, `name`, and `total_goals_scored`. (Only include teams that have played at least one match).
solution: SELECT t.team_id, t.name, SUM(CASE WHEN m.home_team_id = t.team_id THEN m.home_score ELSE m.away_score END) AS total_goals_scored FROM teams t JOIN matches m ON t.team_id = m.home_team_id OR t.team_id = m.away_team_id GROUP BY t.team_id, t.name ORDER BY total_goals_scored DESC;

title: Average Venue Capacity by City
description: For urban planning and event logistics, it's useful to know the average capacity of sports venues in different cities. This helps in assessing infrastructure and potential audience sizes. Retrieve the `city` and the `average_capacity` for venues in each city.
solution: SELECT city, AVG(capacity) AS average_capacity FROM venues GROUP BY city;

title: Player Count Per Team
description: Team managers often need to know the size of their roster to manage player availability and substitutions. This provides a quick overview of team composition. Count the number of players for each team. Retrieve the `team_id`, `name`, and `player_count`.
solution: SELECT t.team_id, t.name, COUNT(p.player_id) AS player_count FROM teams t LEFT JOIN players p ON t.team_id = p.team_id GROUP BY t.team_id, t.name ORDER BY player_count DESC;

title: Goal Scorers
description: The league wants to highlight all players who have contributed to scoring goals, recognizing their offensive contributions. This list can be used for player profiles and statistics. Retrieve the `first_name`, `last_name`, and `team_id` of all players who have scored at least one goal in any match.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT p.first_name, p.last_name, p.team_id FROM players p JOIN stats_log sl ON p.player_id = sl.player_id WHERE sl.goals > 0;

title: Matches Hosted Per Venue
description: Venue management needs to track the usage of their facilities to plan maintenance and future scheduling. This helps in understanding which venues are most active. Count the total number of matches hosted at each venue. Retrieve the `venue_id`, `name`, and `total_matches_hosted`.
solution: SELECT v.venue_id, v.name, COUNT(m.match_id) AS total_matches_hosted FROM venues v LEFT JOIN matches m ON v.venue_id = m.venue_id GROUP BY v.venue_id, v.name ORDER BY total_matches_hosted DESC;

title: Add New Team
description: A new expansion team, 'Phoenix Rising FC', based in 'Phoenix', founded in 2024, is joining the league. This requires updating the `teams` roster. Add this new team with `team_id` 'T010' to the `teams` table.
solution: INSERT INTO teams (team_id, name, city, founded_year) VALUES ('T010', 'Phoenix Rising FC', 'Phoenix', 2024);

description: Due to a tactical change, player 'P005' (e.g., John Doe) has changed their position from 'Midfielder' to 'Defender'. This update is crucial for accurate team sheets and analytics. Update the `position` for player 'P005' in the `players` table.
solution: UPDATE players SET position = 'Defender' WHERE player_id = 'P005';
verifyQuery: SELECT position FROM players WHERE player_id = 'P005';

Delete the record for `match_id` 'M015' from the `matches` table, ensuring any related stats are also removed.
solution: DELETE FROM stats_log WHERE match_id = 'M015'; DELETE FROM matches WHERE match_id = 'M015';

title: Add New Venue
description: A new stadium, 'Grand Arena', with a capacity of 75000, has opened in 'Los Angeles' and will host league matches. This new facility needs to be added to the list of available venues. Add this new venue with `venue_id` 'V005' to the `venues` table.
solution: INSERT INTO venues (venue_id, name, city, capacity) VALUES ('V005', 'Grand Arena', 'Los Angeles', 75000);

title: Relocate Team
description: The 'Team Alpha' (T001) has relocated from 'New York' to 'Boston' for the upcoming season. This change needs to be reflected in the team's official records. Update the `city` for `team_id` 'T001' in the `teams` table.
solution: UPDATE teams SET city = 'Boston' WHERE team_id = 'T001';

description: Team 'T002' has signed a new player, 'Alice Smith', who plays 'Goalkeeper' with jersey number 1. This new addition needs to be recorded in the player roster. Add this player with `player_id` 'P021' to the `players` table, assigned to `team_id` 'T002'.
solution: INSERT INTO players (player_id, team_id, first_name, last_name, position, jersey_number) VALUES ('P021', 'T002', 'Alice', 'Smith', 'Goalkeeper', 1);

title: Correct Match Score
description: An error was found in the score recording for match 'M003'; the actual score was 3-2, not 2-1. It's crucial to maintain accurate match results for league standings. Update the `home_score` to 3 and `away_score` to 2 for `match_id` 'M003'.
solution: UPDATE matches SET home_score = 3, away_score = 2 WHERE match_id = 'M003';

title: Remove Player Stats for a Match

solution: DELETE FROM stats_log WHERE player_id = 'P001' AND match_id = 'M001';

title: Add Coach Name to Teams
description: The league decided to track the head coach for each team to provide more comprehensive team information. This requires an alteration to the existing team structure. Add a new column named `coach_name` of type `TEXT` to the `teams` table.
solution: ALTER TABLE teams ADD COLUMN coach_name TEXT;

title: Rename Venue Capacity Column
description: To improve clarity and specificity, the `capacity` column in the `venues` table should be renamed to `seating_capacity`. This makes the column's purpose more explicit. Rename this column in the `venues` table.
solution: ALTER TABLE venues RENAME COLUMN capacity TO seating_capacity;

title: Remove Founded Year from Teams
description: The `founded_year` column in the `teams` table is no longer considered relevant for current analytics and is cluttering the schema. This column needs to be removed. Drop the `founded_year` column from the `teams` table.

title: Create Referees Table
description: To manage match officials and track their assignments, a new table `referees` is needed. This table will store information about each referee. Create this table with columns `referee_id` (TEXT PRIMARY KEY), `first_name` (TEXT), and `last_name` (TEXT).
solution: CREATE TABLE referees (referee_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT);

title: Add Referee to Matches
description: To track which referee officiated each match, you need to add a `referee_id` column to the `matches` table and establish a foreign key relationship with the `referees` table. First, add the `referee_id` column (TEXT), then add the foreign key constraint to link it to the `referees` table.
solution: ALTER TABLE matches ADD COLUMN referee_id TEXT; ALTER TABLE matches ADD CONSTRAINT fk_referee FOREIGN KEY (referee_id) REFERENCES referees(referee_id);

title: Change Jersey Number Data Type
description: Some players might have non-numeric jersey numbers (e.g., 'GK' for Goalkeeper) which the current INTEGER type cannot accommodate. Modify the `jersey_number` column in the `players` table to be `VARCHAR(5)` to allow for such values. (Note: This may require a table recreation workaround in some SQL dialects like SQLite).
solution: ALTER TABLE players RENAME TO players_old; CREATE TABLE players ( player_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, team_id TEXT, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT, position TEXT, jersey_number VARCHAR(5), FOREIGN KEY (team_id) REFERENCES teams(team_id) ); INSERT INTO players SELECT player_id, team_id, first_name, last_name, position, CAST(jersey_number AS VARCHAR(5)) FROM players_old; DROP TABLE players_old;

title: Remove Referees Table

title: Add Match Status Column
description: To better track the state of matches (e.g., 'Scheduled', 'Completed', 'Cancelled'), add a new column `status` (TEXT) to the `matches` table. This column should have a default value of 'Scheduled' for new entries.
solution: ALTER TABLE matches ADD COLUMN status TEXT DEFAULT 'Scheduled';

title: League Organization
description: Construct tables to manage sports leagues. Define a `leagues` table with `league_id` as an in-line INTEGER PRIMARY KEY and `name` (TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE). Following that, formulate a `teams_new` table holding `team_id` (INTEGER), `team_name` (TEXT NOT NULL), `founded_year` (INTEGER), and `league_id` (INTEGER). Ensure `teams_new` implements an out-of-line PRIMARY KEY for `team_id` and an out-of-line FOREIGN KEY for `league_id` connected to `leagues(league_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE leagues (league_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE); CREATE TABLE teams_new (team_id INTEGER, team_name TEXT NOT NULL, founded_year INTEGER, league_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (team_id), FOREIGN KEY (league_id) REFERENCES leagues(league_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Tournament Registration
description: Build a tournament registration mechanism. Form a `tournaments` table using an in-line `tourney_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY), `name` (TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE), and `prize_pool` (REAL). Subsequently, formulate an `entries` table comprising `entry_id` (INTEGER), `player_name` (TEXT NOT NULL), `fee_paid` (BOOLEAN DEFAULT 0), and `tourney_id` (INTEGER). In `entries`, insert out-of-line rules for a PRIMARY KEY on `entry_id`, a CHECK limiting `fee_paid IN (0, 1)`, and a FOREIGN KEY binding `tourney_id` to `tournaments(tourney_id)`.
solution: CREATE TABLE tournaments (tourney_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, prize_pool REAL); CREATE TABLE entries (entry_id INTEGER, player_name TEXT NOT NULL, fee_paid BOOLEAN DEFAULT 0, tourney_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (entry_id), CHECK (fee_paid IN (0, 1)), FOREIGN KEY (tourney_id) REFERENCES tournaments(tourney_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Athlete Sponsorships
description: Form tables to manage corporate sponsorships. Create a `brands` table with an in-line `brand_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY) and `brand_name` (VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL). Next, produce a `sponsorships` table containing `contract_id` (BIGINT), `athlete_name` (VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL), `contract_value` (DECIMAL(15,2)), and `brand_id` (INTEGER). Apply out-of-line rules in `sponsorships` making `contract_id` the PRIMARY KEY, adding a CHECK constraint for `contract_value > 0`, and establishing a FOREIGN KEY from `brand_id` to `brands(brand_id)`. Separate the statements with a semicolon.
solution: CREATE TABLE brands (brand_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, brand_name VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE sponsorships (contract_id BIGINT, athlete_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, contract_value DECIMAL(15,2), brand_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (contract_id), CHECK (contract_value > 0), FOREIGN KEY (brand_id) REFERENCES brands(brand_id));
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Teams Without Home Wins
description: The league management wants to identify teams that have struggled to win at their home venue, which could indicate a lack of home-field advantage. This requires checking all home matches for each team. Retrieve the `team_id` and `name` of all teams that have never recorded a home win.
solution: SELECT t.team_id, t.name FROM teams t WHERE t.team_id NOT IN (SELECT m.home_team_id FROM matches m WHERE m.home_score > m.away_score);

title: Players with Both Yellow and Red Cards
description: The disciplinary committee is reviewing players with a history of both yellow and red card infractions to understand player discipline. This requires checking `stats_log` for both types of cards. Retrieve the `first_name` and `last_name` of players who have received at least one yellow card and at least one red card across any matches.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT p.first_name, p.last_name FROM players p JOIN stats_log sl_yellow ON p.player_id = sl_yellow.player_id WHERE sl_yellow.yellow_cards > 0 AND p.player_id IN (SELECT sl_red.player_id FROM stats_log sl_red WHERE sl_red.red_cards > 0);

title: Ranked Team Standings
description: Generate a full league table, ranking teams by their total points. Points are awarded as 3 for a win, 1 for a tie, and 0 for a loss, considering both home and away matches. Retrieve the `team_id`, `name`, `total_points`, and their `rank` in the league. Name the result column 'team_rank'.
solution: WITH TeamPoints AS ( SELECT t.team_id, t.name, SUM(CASE WHEN m.home_team_id = t.team_id AND m.home_score > m.away_score THEN 3 WHEN m.away_team_id = t.team_id AND m.away_score > m.home_score THEN 3 WHEN m.home_team_id = t.team_id AND m.home_score = m.away_score THEN 1 WHEN m.away_team_id = t.team_id AND m.away_score = m.home_score THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS total_points FROM teams t JOIN matches m ON t.team_id = m.home_team_id OR t.team_id = m.away_team_id GROUP BY t.team_id, t.name ) SELECT team_id, name, total_points, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_points DESC) AS team_rank FROM TeamPoints ORDER BY team_rank;

title: Top 3 Assist Leaders
description: The league wants to highlight the most creative players by identifying the top 3 players who have accumulated the highest total number of assists across all matches they've played. This recognizes playmaking abilities. Retrieve their `first_name`, `last_name`, and `total_assists`.
solution: SELECT p.first_name, p.last_name, SUM(sl.assists) AS total_assists FROM players p JOIN stats_log sl ON p.player_id = sl.player_id GROUP BY p.player_id, p.first_name, p.last_name ORDER BY total_assists DESC LIMIT 3;

title: High-Usage Venues
description: The operations team needs to identify venues that are frequently used for matches to prioritize maintenance and resource allocation. This helps in managing venue infrastructure effectively. Retrieve the `venue_id`, `name`, and `city` of all venues that have hosted more than 5 matches.
solution: SELECT v.venue_id, v.name, v.city FROM venues v JOIN matches m ON v.venue_id = m.venue_id GROUP BY v.venue_id, v.name, v.city HAVING COUNT(m.match_id) > 5;

title: Most Offensive Team
description: Identify the team with the strongest offense by calculating their average goals scored per match, including both home and away games. This helps in assessing team performance and strategy. Retrieve the `team_id`, `name`, and `average_goals_per_match` for the top team. Name the result column 'total_goals'.
solution: WITH TeamGoals AS ( SELECT t.team_id, t.name, SUM(CASE WHEN m.home_team_id = t.team_id THEN m.home_score ELSE m.away_score END) AS total_goals, COUNT(m.match_id) AS total_matches FROM teams t JOIN matches m ON t.team_id = m.home_team_id OR t.team_id = m.away_team_id GROUP BY t.team_id, t.name ) SELECT team_id, name, CAST(total_goals AS REAL) / total_matches AS average_goals_per_match FROM TeamGoals WHERE total_matches > 0 ORDER BY average_goals_per_match DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Iron Man Players
description: The coaching staff wants to recognize players who have shown exceptional consistency by participating in every single match their team has played. This highlights player durability and importance. Retrieve the `first_name`, `last_name`, and `team_id` of such players. Name the result column 'team_matches'.
solution: SELECT p.first_name, p.last_name, p.team_id FROM players p JOIN (SELECT team_id, COUNT(DISTINCT match_id) AS team_matches FROM (SELECT home_team_id AS team_id, match_id FROM matches UNION ALL SELECT away_team_id AS team_id, match_id FROM matches) GROUP BY team_id) AS team_match_counts ON p.team_id = team_match_counts.team_id JOIN (SELECT player_id, COUNT(DISTINCT match_id) AS player_matches FROM stats_log GROUP BY player_id) AS player_match_counts ON p.player_id = player_match_counts.player_id WHERE player_match_counts.player_matches = team_match_counts.team_matches;

title: Odd Total Goal Matches
description: For a fun statistical quirk, identify all matches where the combined total of home and away goals was an odd number. This can be an interesting data point for sports commentators. Retrieve the `match_id`, `home_score`, `away_score`, and the `total_goals` for these matches.
solution: SELECT match_id, home_score, away_score, (home_score + away_score) AS total_goals FROM matches WHERE (home_score + away_score) % 2 = 1;

title: Integer Division Behavior
description: In SQLite, dividing an integer by another integer results in an integer (e.g., 5 / 2 = 2). To accurately calculate 'goals per minute', we must cast or multiply by a float. For all players who have scored at least 1 goal, find their `player_id` and calculate their goals per minute (which is `goals` / `minutes_played`). Prevent integer division truncating to 0 by multiplying `goals` by 1.0. Name the result `goals_per_minute`.
solution: SELECT player_id, (goals * 1.0) / minutes_played AS goals_per_minute FROM stats_log WHERE goals > 0;

title: Create a Strict Table
description: Create a new table called `accounts` that includes three columns: `account_id` (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY), `email` (TEXT) that must be UNIQUE and NOT NULL, and `balance` (INTEGER) that is protected by a CHECK constraint ensuring it is greater than or equal to 0.
solution: CREATE TABLE accounts (account_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, email TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL, balance INTEGER CHECK(balance >= 0));

title: Respecting Relationships
description: You need to delete the user with `user_id` = 'U1' from the `users` table. Because SQLite foreign key constraints (if enabled) would block this if they have orders, you must first delete all records in the `orders` table where `user` = 'U1', and then delete the user from the `users` table.

title: Price Categories
description: Write a query that returns the `product_name`, `price`, and a new column called `tier` that is 'Premium' if the price is > 100, and 'Standard' otherwise. Order by price descending.
solution: SELECT product_name, price, CASE WHEN price > 100 THEN 'Premium' ELSE 'Standard' END AS tier FROM products ORDER BY price DESC;

title: Compare to Max Price
description: For every product, return its `product_name`, `price`, and the highest price of any product in the store as a new column named `max_price`. Order by `price` descending.
solution: SELECT product_name, price, (SELECT MAX(price) FROM products) AS max_price FROM products ORDER BY price DESC;

title: The Recreation Pattern
description: SQLite `ALTER TABLE` is limited. You must completely replace the `users` table to change its structure. Create `users_new` with the exact same columns as `users` (`user_id` TEXT PRIMARY KEY, `first_name` TEXT, `last_name` TEXT, `address` TEXT, `email` TEXT), plus a new `status` TEXT column. Then INSERT all data from `users` into `users_new`. Then DROP `users`. Then RENAME `users_new` to `users`.
solution: CREATE TABLE users_new (user_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT, address TEXT, email TEXT, status TEXT); INSERT INTO users_new (user_id, first_name, last_name, address, email) SELECT * FROM users; DROP TABLE users; ALTER TABLE users_new RENAME TO users;

title: Using a CTE
description: Use a Common Table Expression (CTE) named `BigOrders` to find all orders where `total_paid` > 500. Then, query from `BigOrders` and join with `users` to return `first_name`, `last_name`, and `total_paid`. Order by `total_paid` descending.
solution: WITH BigOrders AS (SELECT user, total_paid FROM orders WHERE total_paid > 500) SELECT u.first_name, u.last_name, b.total_paid FROM BigOrders b JOIN users u ON b.user = u.user_id ORDER BY b.total_paid DESC;

title: Rank Products by Price
description: Use the `ROW_NUMBER()` window function to assign a rank to each product based on its price, from highest to lowest. Return `product_name`, `price`, and the new column called `rank`.
solution: SELECT product_name, price, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY price DESC) as rank FROM products;

title: Select Specific Columns

description: Create a table named `allergies` with columns: `allergy_id` (TEXT), `patient_id` (TEXT), and `allergy_name` (TEXT).

description: Find all providers whose specialty is either `Cardiology` or `Pediatrics`.

title: CASE WHEN

title: Select Subquery

solution: UPDATE properties SET bedrooms = 4, price = 650000 WHERE property_id = 101;

title: Inline Constraints
description: Create a `zoning_types` table with an `id` INTEGER PRIMARY KEY and a `zone_name` TEXT NOT NULL.
solution: CREATE TABLE zoning_types (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, zone_name TEXT NOT NULL);

title: Relationship Checks
description: Delete all properties with bedrooms = 0 to clean up invalid data.
solution: DELETE FROM properties WHERE bedrooms = 0;

title: Missing Data check
description: Find properties that are missing a latitude constraint by fetching records where latitude IS NULL.
solution: SELECT property_id, address FROM properties WHERE latitude IS NULL;

title: Advanced Subqueries
description: Find properties whose price is higher than the average price among all properties.
solution: SELECT * FROM properties WHERE price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM properties);

title: CASE WHEN
description: Select property_id and a 'luxury_status' column which is 'Luxury' if price > 1000000 else 'Standard'. Do this for properties.
solution: SELECT property_id, CASE WHEN price > 1000000 THEN 'Luxury' ELSE 'Standard' END AS luxury_status FROM properties;

title: Select Subquery
description: Select property addresses and the total number of properties in the database as a column aliased as 'total_props_db'.
solution: SELECT address, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM properties) AS total_props_db FROM properties;

title: CREATE VIEW
description: Create a view named `cheap_properties` to see only properties where price < 200000.
solution: CREATE VIEW cheap_properties AS SELECT * FROM properties WHERE price < 200000;

title: Custom View
description: Create a view named `luxury_properties` showing only properties with price > 1000000.
solution: CREATE VIEW luxury_properties AS SELECT * FROM properties WHERE price > 1000000;

title: Alter View
description: Drop the `luxury_properties` view.
solution: DROP VIEW luxury_properties;
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='view';

title: The Recreation Pattern
description: Recreate `neighborhoods_new` as `neighborhood_id` INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, `neighborhood_name` TEXT. Insert from `neighborhoods`. Drop `neighborhoods`. Rename `neighborhoods_new` to `neighborhoods`.
solution: CREATE TABLE neighborhoods_new (neighborhood_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, neighborhood_name TEXT); INSERT INTO neighborhoods_new (neighborhood_id, neighborhood_name) SELECT neighborhood_id, neighborhood_name FROM neighborhoods; DROP TABLE neighborhoods; ALTER TABLE neighborhoods_new RENAME TO neighborhoods;

title: CTEs (WITH clause)
description: Use a WITH clause named `ExpensiveOnly` to select all properties WHERE price > 1000000, then select all from ExpensiveOnly.
solution: WITH ExpensiveOnly AS (SELECT * FROM properties WHERE price > 1000000) SELECT * FROM ExpensiveOnly;

title: Window Functions
description: Select property addresses and rank them by price descending using ROW_NUMBER. Give the rank column the alias 'rank'.
solution: SELECT address, ROW_NUMBER() OVER(ORDER BY price DESC) AS rank FROM properties;

title: Drop Table
description: Drop the 'property_features' table if it exists.
solution: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS property_features;
verifyQuery: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Inner Join: Flights and Aircraft
description: The operations team needs a comprehensive view of flights along with the model of the aircraft assigned to them. Retrieve the flight_id from the flights table and the model from the aircraft table for all flights using an INNER JOIN.
solution: SELECT f.flight_id, a.model FROM flights f JOIN aircraft a ON f.aircraft_id = a.aircraft_id;

title: Inner Join: Properties and Neighborhoods
description: Retrieve the address of each property along with its neighborhood_name by joining the properties table and the neighborhoods table. A property belongs to a neighborhood if its latitude and longitude fall between the neighborhood's min/max latitude and longitude.
solution: SELECT p.address, n.neighborhood_name FROM properties p JOIN neighborhoods n ON p.latitude BETWEEN n.min_latitude AND n.max_latitude AND p.longitude BETWEEN n.min_longitude AND n.max_longitude;

title: Revenue Percentiles
description: Calculate the percent_rank of employees based on their salaries in each department using Window Functions.
solution: SELECT employee_id, department_id, salary, PERCENT_RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary) as pct_rank FROM employees;

title: Patient Visit Frequency Rankings
description: Rank patients by the total number of visits they have had, partitioned by their primary doctor using DENSE_RANK().
solution: SELECT patient_id, doctor_id, count(visit_id) as total_visits, DENSE_RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY doctor_id ORDER BY count(visit_id) DESC) as visit_rank FROM visits GROUP BY patient_id, doctor_id;

title: Common Table Expression Basics
description: Use a CTE named 'recent_observations' to select observations from 2023, then select all from the CTE.
solution: WITH recent_observations AS (SELECT * FROM observations WHERE date >= '2023-01-01') SELECT * FROM recent_observations;

title: Running Total of Credits
description: Calculate the running total of credits for a course schedule using the SUM() window function ordered by course_id.
solution: SELECT course_id, credits, SUM(credits) OVER(ORDER BY course_id) as running_total FROM courses;

title: Introduction to SELECT Statements
description: As the new HR reporting lead, you need to verify the departments configured in our retail system. Retrieve the ID, name, and budget of every department to map out our cost centers.
solution: SELECT * FROM departments;

title: Selecting Specific Columns
description: The marketing team wants to print customized name tags for an upcoming all-hands store meeting. Return a streamlined list containing only the first_name and last_name of all employees.
solution: SELECT first_name, last_name FROM employees;

title: Filtering Rows with WHERE
description: You need to identify all employees hired in 2024 to send them an anniversary gift and distribute revised stock options. Find the first name, last name, and hire date of employees whose hire_date begins with the year '2024'.
solution: SELECT * FROM payrolls WHERE salary > 50000;

title: Creating Your First Table
description: Before pushing a system-wide update to the HR portal, the IT team needs a hard count of user licenses. How many employees exist in the human resources system? Alias as 'employee_count'.
solution: CREATE TABLE benefits (id INTEGER, name TEXT);

title: Adding Data with INSERT
description: We need to mail physical corporate packets to each division. List each employee's first name, last name, and the text name of their department using an INNER JOIN between the employees and departments tables.
solution: INSERT INTO departments (department_id, name) VALUES (99, 'Training');

title: Deleting Basic Records
description: Does performance truly translate into pay? Find the average bonus received by employees for each performance rating. Group by rating and return the rating alongside the average bonus aliased as avg_bonus.
solution: DELETE FROM departments WHERE department_id = 99;

title: Sorting Data with ORDER BY
description: To track compensation inflation over the history of the company, calculate a rolling average of all salaries ordered historically by the employee's hire date. Return the first name, hire date, salary, and the rolling average.
solution: SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY hire_date DESC;

title: Limiting Results
description: The CFO needs a quick snapshot of upper-management compensation. Retrieve the top 5 highest salaries from the payrolls table so she can review the highest expenditure items.
solution: SELECT * FROM payrolls ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 5;

title: Finding Unique Values with DISTINCT
description: Recruitment is auditing our current organizational hierarchy. Produce a clean, distinct list of all job titles currently held across the entire company.
solution: SELECT DISTINCT title FROM employees;

title: Filtering with MULTIPLE Values (IN)
description: The regional manager for our primary hubs wants a consolidated employee list. Use the IN operator to retrieve everyone working in department 1 (Sales) or department 2 (HR).
solution: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id IN (1, 2);

title: Filtering within Ranges (BETWEEN)
description: To ensure pay equity across our mid-level management, HR demands a report on standard salary bands. Identify all payroll records where the salary falls strictly between 40,000 and 60,000.
solution: SELECT * FROM payrolls WHERE salary BETWEEN 40000 AND 60000;

title: Pattern Matching with LIKE
description: A customer left positive feedback about an employee whose name started with 'A', but they couldn't remember the full name. Find all employees whose first name begins with 'A' to help track them down.
solution: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE first_name LIKE 'A%';

title: Combining Filters (AND)
description: Due to changing labor laws regarding shift workers, the legal team needs a specific cohort. Find all employees who are categorized as 'Part-Time' AND are paid 'Hourly'.
solution: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE employment_type = 'Part-Time' AND pay_type = 'Hourly';

title: Alternative Filters (OR)
description: For an upcoming policy update regarding overtime exemptions, we need a list of exempt personnel. Find all employees who are either paid by 'Salary' OR hold the title of 'Store Manager'.
solution: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE pay_type = 'Salary' OR title = 'Store Manager';

title: Counting Rows (COUNT)
description: The CFO expects a top-down view of our retail allocations. Calculate the total budgeted amount for all departments combined and alias the result as 'total_budget'.
solution: SELECT count(*) FROM employees;

title: Totaling Values (SUM)
description: HR is comparing our firm's basic compensation against industry standards. Find out the average salary given out across the company and alias it as 'avg_salary'.
solution: SELECT sum(budget) FROM departments;

title: Averages and Maximums
description: We are flagging high-flyers who are out-earning the baseline. Leverage a subquery to identify employees who earn strictly more than the overall company average salary, returning their first name, last name, and salary.
solution: SELECT AVG(salary), MAX(salary) FROM payrolls;

title: Grouping Results (GROUP BY)
description: We need to see who is the most expensive asset within each specific department. Use a Window Function to RANK employees by highest salary within their department partitions. Return employee_id, department_id, salary, and their specific rank as salary_rank.
solution: SELECT pay_type, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY pay_type;

title: Filtering Groups (HAVING)
description: We are looking for departments that are properly staffed, rather than empty shell codes or solo operators. Show the department IDs that contain strictly more than 1 employee.
solution: SELECT department_id, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

title: Updating Existing Records
description: Some team members might be stuck in an onboarding state without a department assigned, causing payroll glitches. Use a LEFT JOIN from employees to departments to list everyone's first and last name, alongside their department name (which will remain NULL if unassigned).
solution: UPDATE payrolls SET bonus = 500 WHERE employee_id = 1;

title: Updating Multiple Fields
description: Employee 2 has received a promotion! In a single command, update their payroll record: set their bonus to 1000 and permanently raise their base salary to 50000.
solution: UPDATE payrolls SET bonus = 1000, salary = 50000 WHERE employee_id = 2;

title: Conditional Deletions
description: We are archiving outdated negative reviews to give team members a fresh start for the new year. Delete all performance reviews that hold a rating of less than 3.
solution: DELETE FROM performance_reviews WHERE rating < 3;

title: Altering Tables (Adding Columns)
description: The VP of Retail Operations needs a directory of physical locations alongside leadership. List the city of each store along with the first and last name of its manager by joining stores and employees.
solution: ALTER TABLE employees ADD COLUMN middle_name TEXT;

title: Dropping Tables
description: We are migrating entirely to a new geographic management system, deprecating the old store location tracking. Permanently drop the stores table from the database.
solution: DROP TABLE stores;

title: Introduction to Table Relationships (INNER JOIN)
description: Executive management has requested a quick roster of all current retail leadership. Retrieve the employee IDs and names of all staff currently holding the 'Store Manager' title.
solution: SELECT employees.first_name, departments.name FROM employees INNER JOIN departments ON employees.department_id = departments.department_id;

title: Enforcing Table Architecture (Foreign Keys)
description: The IT team needs to track company laptops and phones given out to staff. Create an 'assets' table with an id, and enforce strict relational integrity by linking an employee_id column to the employees table.
solution: CREATE TABLE assets (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, employee_id INTEGER, FOREIGN KEY(employee_id) REFERENCES employees(employee_id));

title: Inline Data Constraints (NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK)
description: We are standardizing our corporate titles to prevent typos like 'Sales Assoc' vs 'Sales Associate'. Create a 'roles' table where the 'title' column is both UNIQUE and cannot be NULL.
solution: CREATE TABLE roles (title TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL);

title: Data Integrity (Constraint Violations / Cascades)
description: To test the security of our newly established constraints, intentionally attempt to insert a NULL value into the roles table and observe the resulting database error.
solution: INSERT INTO roles (title) VALUES (NULL);

title: Including Unmatched Records (LEFT JOIN)
description: It's review time! The system flags top performers for a potential automatic bonus trigger. Find all performance reviews that have a perfect rating of 5, selecting the review ID and employee ID.
solution: SELECT employees.first_name, departments.name FROM employees LEFT JOIN departments ON employees.department_id = departments.department_id;

title: Multi-Table Chains
description: For the executive dashboard, we need a complete picture of personnel costs. Chain together employees, departments, and payrolls to display first names, department names, and final salaries side-by-side.
solution: SELECT e.first_name, d.name, p.salary FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id JOIN payrolls p ON e.employee_id = p.employee_id;

title: Finding Missing Data (IS NULL)
description: HR suspects an error where some contractors were onboarded without active payment profiles. Left join employees to payrolls and find the team members whose payroll_id remains purely NULL.
solution: SELECT e.first_name FROM employees e LEFT JOIN payrolls p ON e.employee_id = p.employee_id WHERE p.payroll_id IS NULL;

title: Filtering using Subqueries (Left Join/Subquery combos)
description: We want to isolate the employees belonging to our most heavily funded divisions. Use a nested subquery in the WHERE clause to retrieve only employees whose department has a budget exceeding 2,000,000.
solution: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id IN (SELECT department_id FROM departments WHERE budget > 2000000);

title: Advanced Subquery Comparisons
description: Identify the top earners driving our payroll expenditures. Produce a list of all payroll records where the salary is mathematically greater than the overall company-wide average salary.
solution: SELECT * FROM payrolls WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM payrolls);

title: Conditional Logic with CASE WHEN
description: We are generating simple access badges. Use a CASE WHEN statement to categorize anyone with the 'Store Manager' title as a 'Manager', and everybody else as 'Staff', aliased nicely as role_type.
solution: SELECT first_name, CASE WHEN title = 'Store Manager' THEN 'Manager' ELSE 'Staff' END as role_type FROM employees;

title: Subqueries in the SELECT Clause
description: To show employees how they stack up against the absolute ceiling, retrieve their first name and use an inline SELECT subquery to display the absolute maximum company salary directly next to it.
solution: SELECT first_name, (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM payrolls) as max_company_salary FROM employees;

title: Composite Primary Keys and Out-of-Line Constraints
description: We are rolling out an intense holiday scheduling system. Create a 'shift_assignments' table utilizing a composite primary key formed from both employee_id and store_id to prevent redundant shifts.
solution: CREATE TABLE shift_assignments (employee_id INTEGER, store_id INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (employee_id, store_id));

title: Advanced Schema Planning
description: Before we migrate the schema, we need an automated dump of our tables. Write a query against the system dictionary (sqlite_master) to simply list the names of all currently created tables.
solution: -- Schema planning is conceptual. Write a simple query to list all tables. \n SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table';

title: Speeding Up Lookups (CREATE INDEX)
description: The system is slowing down whenever HR searches by employee roles. Help optimize our database performance by creating a targeted index named idx_emp_title on the employee title column.
solution: CREATE INDEX idx_emp_title ON employees(title);

title: Saving Complex Queries (CREATE VIEW)
description: We frequently run reports on the executive tier. Save us time by creating a virtual view called HighEarners that permanently encapsulates a query fetching payrolls with salaries over 80,000.
solution: CREATE VIEW HighEarners AS SELECT * FROM payrolls WHERE salary > 80000;

title: Building Custom Views
description: The accounting firm needs to see departments and their associated budgets without exposing the raw underlying table. Create a DeptBudgets view encompassing the department ID, name, and budget.
solution: CREATE VIEW DeptBudgets AS SELECT department_id, name, budget FROM departments;

title: Altering Existing Views
description: Management has decided to change the definition of the HighEarners view to include bonuses. As a first step directly supported by SQLite, execute a DROP VIEW IF EXISTS to clear the old HighEarners definition.
solution: DROP VIEW IF EXISTS HighEarners;

title: The Recreation Pattern for Complex Modfications
description: We need to fundamentally change the column types in our departments table, but SQLite blocks direct mutation. Kick off the recreation pattern by renaming the existing departments table to old_departments.
solution: ALTER TABLE departments RENAME TO old_departments;

title: Common Table Expressions (CTEs/WITH clauses)
description: Let's perform some advanced abstraction. Use a CTE named Managers to cleanly isolate the employee IDs of Store Managers, then select all data purely from that CTE.
solution: WITH Mangers AS (SELECT employee_id FROM employees WHERE title='Store Manager') SELECT * FROM Mangers;

title: Window Functions
description: We want a sorted leaderboard of salaries without losing individual row data. Deploy a window function over the payrolls table to create a clean ranking metric (salary_rank) sorted by descending salary.
solution: SELECT employee_id, salary, RANK() OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC) as salary_rank FROM payrolls;

title: Introduction to Common Table Expressions
description: Use a CTE to select Store Managers, then query everything from the CTE.
solution: WITH Mangers AS (SELECT employee_id FROM employees WHERE title='Store Manager') SELECT * FROM Mangers;
